At what point does a developing human cross the line and acquire moral interests?
The answer determines when a fetus gains a moral claim to protection.
This claim can conflict with the bodily autonomy of the pregnant person.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The Four Candidate Criteria


1. Biological Humanity
Moral status begins at fertilization, coextensive with membership in the species Homo sapiens.
2. Sentience
Moral status begins when the developing organism can experience pain or pleasure (approx. 24 weeks).



3. Personhood
Moral status is reserved for beings possessing advanced cognitive capacities like rationality and self-awareness.
4. Viability
Moral status begins when the fetus can survive outside the uterus (approx. 22-24 weeks).

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Concept Map: Moral Status Criteria


Overview of bioethical criteria.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Criterion 1: Biological Humanity


The Core Claim

Moral status is coextensive with being a member of the species Homo sapiens.
A human zygote, embryo, and fetus possess full moral status from fertilization onward.
Abortion is morally equivalent to killing an adult human being.




Major Criticisms

Speciesism: Accused of placing absolute value on genetic category alone.
Potentiality Fallacy: Relies too heavily on the potential of a single cell to become a person.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Criterion 2: Sentience


The Core Claim

Moral status belongs to beings capable of experiencing pleasure and pain (Singer 2011).
An entity without conscious experiences has no interests to protect; once it feels, it counts.
Thalamocortical connections capabable of processing pain do not function until approx. 24 weeks.




Implications & Criticisms

Early Permissibility: Early abortions do not violate any interests of the fetus.
Non-Human Animals: Extends moral status to animals while excluding early embryos.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Criterion 3: Personhood


The Core Claim

Defines a person by advanced cognitive capacities: reasoning, self-awareness, and agency.
Being biologically human is not enough; one must possess a mind that can plan and communicate.
Defended by liberal philosophers like Michael Tooley and Mary Anne Warren.




Implications & Criticisms

High Permissibility: Fetus does not possess personhood, making abortions permissible.
The Infanticide Objection: Excludes infants and individuals with advanced cognitive decline.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Criterion 4: Viability


The Core Claim

Draws the moral line at the ability of the fetus to survive outside the uterus.
Before viability, the fetus is entirely dependent on the pregnant person’s body to exist.
Once viable, the fetus has physical independence that grounds a claim to life.




Legal Bounds & Criticisms

Legal Landmark: Served as the core boundary in Roe v. Wade and Planned Parenthood v. Casey.
Tech Dependency: Viability is highly dependent on medical technology, not the fetus itself.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Mary Anne Warren: Biological vs. Moral Sense
text:
Mary Anne Warren: Biological vs. Moral Sense

In her 1973 landmark paper “On the Moral and Legal Status of Abortion,” Mary Anne Warren defends a liberal approach to abortion (Warren 1973).
She argues that the abortion debate often founders because both sides conflate the two different senses of the word “human.”
The biological sense of human refers simply to being a genetically human organism (a member of the species Homo sapiens).
The moral sense of human refers to being a member of the moral community who possesses full moral rights (a person).
Warren argues that having moral status requires being a person in the moral sense, not just a biological human.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The Five Criteria of Personhood



Consciousness: The capacity to feel pain and experience the external world.
Reasoning: The developed cognitive capacity to solve new, complex problems.
Self-motivated activity: The capacity to initiate actions independent of external control.



Communication: The capacity to transmit messages of an indefinite variety.
Self-awareness: Possessing a concept of oneself as an individual persisting over time.







Definition of Personhood


Warren proposes that satisfying some or all of these five cognitive criteria is what makes an entity a person (Warren 1973).

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Personhood and the Fetus

A human fetus in early development does not satisfy any of the five cognitive criteria of personhood.
Therefore, Warren concludes that the fetus is not a person in the moral community.
As a non-person, the fetus has no moral rights that can override the bodily rights of the pregnant person.
This view represents a highly consistent, cognitive-based defense of extreme abortion permissibility.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Potential Personhood & The Space Explorer


The Dilemma

Imagine an explorer captured by aliens who plan to split their body into thousands of pieces to create clones.
Each clone is a potential person who would have a valuable future right to life.
The explorer can escape only by destroying the cloning machine and all potential clones.




Warren’s Solution

The explorer is fully justified in escaping to preserve their actual life and liberty.
By escaping, the explorer destroys thousands of potential lives.
Warren concludes that the rights of an actual person (the pregnant individual) always override the claims of a potential person (the fetus).

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The Objection from Infanticide

The most common objection to Warren’s criteria is that newborns also fail to satisfy most of her five criteria for personhood.
If her personhood criteria are correct, it would seem to imply that infanticide is morally permissible, which is widely rejected.
Warren responds by arguing that infanticide is wrong for reasons other than the infant’s intrinsic moral status (Warren 1973).
First, a newborn child is highly valued by society, and other people are eager to adopt and care for it, meaning killing it deprives others of value.
Second, once birth has occurred, the infant no longer violates the bodily autonomy or physical resources of the biological mother.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The Violinist Thought Experiment


The Scenario

You wake up connected to a famous, unconscious violinist who has a fatal kidney disease (Thomson 1971).
The Society of Music Lovers kidnapped you and plugged your circulatory system into his.
Unplugging him kills him, but staying connected for nine months allows him to recover.




The Core Question

Are you morally required to remain connected for nine months to save his life?
Thomson argues that you have no obligation to support him, even if it is generous to do so.
She concedes that a fetus is a person from conception for the sake of argument.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The Violinist Analogy


“You wake up in the morning and find yourself back to back in bed with an unconscious violinist. A famous unconscious violinist. He has been found to have a fatal kidney ailment, and the Society of Music Lovers has canvassed all the available medical records and found that you alone have the right blood type to help. They have therefore kidnapped you, and last night the violinist’s circulatory system was plugged into yours, so that your kidneys can be used to extract poisons from his blood as well as your own. The director of the hospital now tells you,”Look, we’re sorry the Society of Music Lovers did this to you–we would never have permitted it if we had known. But still, they did it, and the violinist is now plugged into you. To unplug you would be to kill him. But never mind, it’s only for nine months. By then he will have recovered from his ailment, and can safely be unplugged from you.” Is it morally incumbent on you to accede to this situation? No doubt it would be very nice of you if you did, a great kindness. But do you have to accede to it?”


Judith Jarvis Thomson, “A Defense of Abortion” (Thomson 1971).

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Bodily Autonomy vs. Right to Life
text:
Bodily Autonomy vs. Right to Life


Bodily Consent

The violinist has a right to life, but not a right to use your kidneys without your ongoing consent.
A right to life does not include a blank check to another’s bodily resources (Thomson 1971).
In pregnancy, survival requires the continuous physical support of the pregnant person.




Withdrawing Support

Unplugging the violinist is not an act of unjust killing; it is withdrawing a benefit he has no right to demand.
Similarly, terminating a pregnancy is not a violation of the fetus’s right to life, but a withdrawal of bodily consent.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Objections and Counters







Common Objection
Thomson’s Likely Counter




Rape vs. Consensual Sex: The violinist scenario only applies to pregnancy resulting from rape, not consensual intercourse.
Consensual sex is not a blank check; opening a window does not give a burglar the right to stay in your house.


Killing vs. Letting Die: Unplugging the violinist lets him die of natural disease, but abortion actively kills a fetus.
The moral core is identical: you are removing a body from yours; you are not obligated to keep them alive.


Parental Duty: Parents have special obligations to their offspring that strangers do not have to violinists.
We do not have natural, unchosen duties to support others at major physical cost; responsibility must be assumed.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The People-Seeds Thought Experiment

Thomson addresses the claim that voluntary, consensual intercourse implies consent to fetal bodily support.
She designs the people-seeds thought experiment: imagine microscopic human seeds floating in the air and taking root in your carpets (Thomson 1971).
To protect your house, you install fine window screens; however, a rare screen defect allows a seed to drift in and grow.
Thomson argues you have no moral duty to keep the person-plant in your home; consensual sex with contraception does not constitute bodily consent.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Right to Life vs. Unjust Killing
text:
Right to Life vs. Unjust Killing

Thomson argues a “right to life” does not mean a right to the minimum resources needed to survive, such as another’s organs.
If you are dying of a rare disease, you have no moral right to demand that a stranger donate their kidney to save you.
Therefore, Thomson defines the right to life strictly as the right not to be killed unjustly.
Withdrawing your bodily resources is not unjust killing; it is simply retracting a voluntary benefit the other has no right to demand.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Samaritans: Good vs. Minimally Decent


The Splendid Samaritan

Goes far out of their way to make massive personal and physical sacrifices to save another.
No laws in the United States force ordinary citizens to perform Splendid Samaritanism (e.g. donating a kidney).




The Minimally Decent Samaritan

Performs the basic, standard level of assistance that any decent human would provide.
US laws do not require even this (e.g., you aren’t legally forced to call 911 for a drowning stranger).








Thomson’s Conclusion


Restrictive abortion laws force pregnant women to make massive physical sacrifices (Splendid Samaritanism) that are never legally demanded of any other citizen.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Autonomy: Indecent vs. Unjust


The Distinction

While defending the right to abort, Thomson notes that having a right does not make an action moral.
Unjust: Violating a person’s strict right (e.g., taking back a gift you promised to let them keep).
Indecent/Callow: Acting without basic virtue, charity, or decency, even if no rights are violated.




Case Illustrations

Violinist Case: If you only needed to remain connected for one hour, unplugging him would be callow and highly indecent.
Pregnancy Case: Terminating a late-stage pregnancy simply to avoid rescheduling a vacation is indecent and selfish.
In both cases, the act remains morally wrong because it fails the test of basic decency.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Don Marquis on Why Killing Is Wrong
text:
Don Marquis on Why Killing Is Wrong

In 1989, Don Marquis published “Why Abortion Is Immoral” (Marquis 1989).
He argued that the abortion debate is stalled because both sides rely on arbitrary claims.
To resolve it, he asks a fundamental question: why is it wrong to kill an ordinary adult?
The wrongness of killing does not lie in its effect on the killer, nor on the grief of survivors.
The primary wrong of killing is what it does to the victim.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: The Fetus and a Valuable Future
text:
The Fetus and a Valuable Future


“What primarily makes killing wrong is neither its effect on the murderer nor its effect on the victim’s friends and relatives, but its effect on the victim. The loss of one’s life is one of the greatest losses one can suffer. The loss of one’s life deprives one of all the experiences, activities, projects, and enjoyments which would otherwise have constituted one’s future. Therefore, killing someone is wrong, primarily because the killing inflicts (one of) the greatest possible losses on the victim.”


Don Marquis, “Why Abortion is Immoral” (Marquis 1989).

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The FLO Account of Abortion


The Core Concept

Killing is wrong because it deprives a victim of a future-like-ours (FLO): a future of experiences, enjoyments, and projects.
A human fetus possesses a future of value just like any young child or typical adult.




The Moral Conclusion

Aborting a fetus deprives it of this valuable future.
Therefore, abortion is seriously wrong for the exact same reason that killing an adult is wrong.
This argument requires no claims about personhood or religious concepts of the soul.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Implications of the FLO Account


Theoretical Virtues

Non-Speciesist: Implies that killing intelligent extraterrestrials with valuable futures is wrong.
Tragedy of Youth: Explains why premature death of infants/children is exceptionally tragic.
Euthanasia: Permits active euthanasia when a patient lacks any prospect of future valuable experiences.




Clinical Abortion Impact

Makes almost all abortions seriously immoral, regardless of the gestational age of the fetus.
The moral weight is carried entirely by the future value the fetus would eventually experience.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Objections to the FLO Argument







Common Objection
Marquis’s Defender Counter




The Potentiality Fallacy: Just because a fetus has potential, that does not give it actual rights now.
The argument is about loss, not rights. Depriving an entity of a future it will experience is a real loss today.


The Interest Objection: An entity cannot be harmed by a loss if it lacks the cognitive capacity to care about it.
A comatose patient can be harmed by being killed, even if they are temporarily unable to care about their future.


Contraception: If FLO is true, then contraception must be wrong because it prevents a valuable future.
Contraception is not killing; there is no identifiable individual who suffers a loss before fertilization.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The “Worst of Crimes” Thesis

Marquis argues that his Future-Like-Ours (FLO) account provides the best explanation for why killing is widely considered the worst of crimes.
When you steal from someone, you deprive them of some of their property, but they still have their lives and other projects.
When you kill someone, you deprive them of everything they have and will ever have—their entire future of experiences.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
FLO and Moral Gravity

The FLO account explains why we believe premature death is a particularly tragic and devastating loss for young people (they lose more future).
It also explains why we do not consider it wrong to kill a person who is brain-dead, as they no longer have a future of conscious experience.
Therefore, the moral gravity of killing is directly proportional to the value of the future of which the victim is deprived.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The Desire Account Objection

Critics argue killing is wrong only because it frustrates the victim’s strong desire to continue living.
Under this view, since a fetus has no cognitive capacity to form desires, abortion is not wrong.
Marquis responds that this account fails to explain why it is seriously wrong to kill depressed, suicidal, or comatose people.
We protect these lives because their futures remain valuable, regardless of their current conscious desires.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The Valuing Account Objection

Another major objection holds that a future is only valuable if it is currently valued by the entity itself.
Marquis counters that a person can be deprived of a valuable future even if they do not currently recognize its value.
Consider a suicidal teenager: if we prevent their suicide and they go on to live a happy life, we have saved a future that became valuable.
Similarly, a fetus’s future is valuable because it will eventually grow to experience and value it, making early termination a real loss.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Identity & IVF Pre-Embryos

The most powerful challenge to Marquis is the identity objection: a fetus must survive as the same continuous individual to lose “its” future (Kamm 1992).
However, during the first 14 days after fertilization, the multicellular zygote can split into identical twins.
If a zygote splits, the original entity ceases to exist; therefore, no stable, single individual exists before day 14 to whom a “future” belongs.
This objection suggests that the FLO argument does not apply to early-stage pre-implantation embryos in IVF clinics.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Rosalind Hursthouse’s Virtue Theory

In 1991, Rosalind Hursthouse published “Virtue Theory and Abortion” (Hursthouse 1991).
She argued that both sides of the debate focus too much on rigid rights and rules.
Rights-based bioethics asks: “what are my rights?” or “what does the law allow?”.
Hursthouse argues these questions miss the emotional, biological, and personal reality of pregnancy.
Virtue ethics asks a different question: “how does a virtuous person act in this situation?”.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Pregnancy as a Grave Moral Fact
text:
Pregnancy as a Grave Moral Fact

Hursthouse argues that pregnancy is not just a physical state like having a cold, nor is it an inert piece of property.
Pregnancy is a significant human biological and existential event, tied to love, family, and mortality.
Approaching abortion with casual disregard reflects a vice: a lack of seriousness or respect for life.
A virtuous agent recognizes that ending a pregnancy is a grave decision that requires serious justification.
It is possible to have a legal right to an abortion while still acting viciously in exercising that right.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Virtue, Vice, and Decisions

Virtue ethics does not yield a simple, single rule (“always wrong” or “always right”).
The morality of an abortion depends on the character, motives, and circumstances of the person making the choice.
Terminating a pregnancy due to severe threat to health, or inability to care for existing children, can be wise and compassionate.
Terminating a pregnancy because it would interfere with a planned vacation reflects selfishness or lack of appreciation for life.
The focus is on flourishing: does this decision align with a life lived with wisdom, courage, and responsibility?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Hursthouse: Rights vs. Virtue


The Limits of “Rights-Mongering”

Bioethics focuses too much on abstract entitlements (“what does the law allow?” or “what is my right?”).
Having a legal or moral right to do something does not automatically make the decision wise, compassionate, or virtuous.
Example: You have a clear legal right to speak rudely to family, but doing so reflects a vicious character.




The Shift to Virtue Ethics

Redirects the debate from abstract rules to the actual character, motives, and circumstances of the moral agent.
Asks a richer clinical and personal question: “how does a virtuous person act in this specific scenario?”.
Treats terminating a pregnancy not as a minor transaction, but as a grave existential decision requiring mature justification.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Virtue and “Appropriate Grief”


Acknowledging Complexity

Unlike rights-based frameworks that treat abortion as either simple or neutral, virtue theory respects moral complexity.
A virtuous agent will experience appropriate grief or sorrow, even when a termination is fully justified.
Sorrow is a sign of a character that appreciates the gravity of human life, potential, and loss.








Case Study: Maternal Threat


A mother terminates a pregnancy because severe heart disease carries a 30% risk of maternal death.

Her decision is wise and compassionate.
Yet, it remains a tragic loss of potential.
Casual indifference to this loss reflects a vice of callowness.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Pregnancy & Human Flourishing


Existential Grounding

Human pregnancy, parenting, and family are tied to biological reproduction and flourishing.
Carrying a pregnancy to term is a major existential decision about one’s role in the cycle of life.
Approaching pregnancy with serious reflection reflects appreciation for the gravity of bringing new life.




The Path to Eudaimonia

Deciding whether to become a parent is a primary component of a well-lived human life (eudaimonia).
Parenting requires years of care and shapes one’s character, virtues, and maternal/paternal duties.
A virtuous agent rejects casual utility in favor of a life lived with wisdom, responsibility, and courage.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Comparing the Four Frameworks










Feature
Warren (Personhood)
Thomson (Bodily Autonomy)
Marquis (Future-Like-Ours)
Hursthouse (Virtue Ethics)




Focal Point
Presence of cognitive capabilities.
Conflict of rights and bodily control.
The loss suffered by the victim.
The character and motives of the agent.


Fetal Status
Lacks personhood; not a moral person.
Conceded as a person for the sake of argument.
Possesses a future of value; status is high.
Irrelevant; what matters is the gravity of life.


Permissibility
Permissible (fetuses have no rights overriding a person).
Often permissible (consent to bodily use is key).
Rarely permissible (except in extreme conflicts).
Context-dependent (wise vs. callow reasons).


Primary Limitation
Leads to difficult objections regarding infants (infanticide).
Relies on analogies that may not match parent-child relations.
Hard to apply to cases of severe genetic anomalies.
Does not provide clear-cut rules for public policy.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Thought Question

A patient with severe heart disease is pregnant. Her doctor tells her that continuing the pregnancy carries a 30% risk of maternal death. She has two young children at home.

How would Thomson, Marquis, and Hursthouse analyze this scenario?
How do the patient’s existing maternal responsibilities change the moral equation for each philosopher?
In a hospital, who is the primary moral agent in this decision: the patient, the doctor, or the nurse?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Make It Contemporary

Severe fetal anomalies incompatible with life (such as anencephaly) present deep moral dilemmas.

In many jurisdictions, restrictive laws place narrow exceptions on late-term abortions.
Clinicians sometimes face legal penalties if they intervene before an active emergency occurs.
Families often want to terminate to avoid suffering, while some suggest carrying to term is required.

How do our three philosophical frameworks help a clinician navigate a situation where legal boundaries, professional ethics, and patient wishes collide?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Three final distinctions to carry forward into your clinical practice:

A right to act is not the same as a virtuous choice. You may have a legal or moral right to withdraw bodily support (Thomson), but doing so for callow reasons still reflects a lack of virtue (Hursthouse).
Tragedies are real. Marquis’s account forces us to acknowledge the genuine loss of a future, even when a termination is fully justified to save a maternal life.
Clinicians support persons, not just arguments. In the hospital, your job is to understand which ethical framework is guiding your patient’s reasoning, rather than imposing your own.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Mini-Recap

Part A laid out the structural biology and major philosophical arguments surrounding abortion. While biological development is continuous, philosophers draw sharp moral boundaries using criteria like biological humanity, sentience, personhood, and viability. Thomson defends abortion by highlighting bodily autonomy; Marquis opposes it by showing that abortion deprives a fetus of a valuable future; Hursthouse redirects the debate toward the character of the moral agent and the gravity of the decision. Part B will build on these ideas to explore how these ethical tensions have shaped the history of abortion law, the post-Dobbs landscape, and the application of the four principles at the bedside.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Key Terms

Zygote — The single-celled organism formed by fertilization.
Embryo — The developing human from implantation through 8 weeks.
Fetus — The developing human from 9 weeks until birth.
Viability — The point where survival outside the womb is possible.
Moral Status — The property of being an entity whose interests count morally.
Personhood — Having advanced cognitive and rational capacities.
Sentience — The capacity to feel pleasure and pain.
Bodily Autonomy — The right to control one’s own physical body.
Future-Like-Ours — Marquis’s term for a valuable future of experiences.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Further Reading

Dworkin, Ronald. Life’s Dominion: An Argument About Abortion, Euthanasia, and Individual Freedom. Vintage, 1994.
Hursthouse, Rosalind. On Virtue Ethics. Oxford University Press, 1999.
Kamm, Frances M. Creation and Abortion: A Study in Moral and Legal Philosophy. Oxford University Press, 1992.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Review Questions
Write a brief answer to each, or use them as discussion prompts.


Recall
Briefly define the four candidate criteria for moral status (biological humanity, sentience, personhood, viability) and name one philosopher or framework associated with each.


Apply
A couple undergoing IVF must decide what to do with remaining, frozen pre-implantation embryos. Analyze the moral gravity of discarding these embryos using two different moral-status criteria (e.g., biological humanity vs. sentience), explaining how each criterion leads to a different ethical conclusion.


Debate
Should legal regulations governing medical procedures like abortion be anchored in a specific moral-status criterion (such as sentience or viability), or should public policy remain neutral on these deep, unresolved metaphysical disputes?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
References


Hursthouse, Rosalind. 1991. “Virtue Theory and Abortion.” Philosophy & Public Affairs 20 (3): 223–46. https://www.jstor.org/stable/2265432.


Kamm, Frances M. 1992. Creation and Abortion: A Study in Moral and Legal Philosophy. Oxford University Press.


Marquis, Don. 1989. “Why Abortion Is Immoral.” The Journal of Philosophy 86 (4): 183–202. https://doi.org/10.2307/2026961.


Singer, Peter. 2011. Practical Ethics. 3rd ed. Cambridge University Press.


Thomson, Judith Jarvis. 1971. “A Defense of Abortion.” Philosophy & Public Affairs 1 (1): 47–66. https://www.jstor.org/stable/2265091.


Warren, Mary Anne. 1973. “On the Moral and Legal Status of Abortion.” The Monist 57 (1): 43–61. https://doi.org/10.5840/monist197357133.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
You have metastatic cancer. Your oncologist says:

“The standard drug has stopped working.”
“There is a study you could join, but I need to be clear: it is a randomized trial.”
“One group gets the current standard treatment. The other gets the new drug.”
“I do not get to choose your group, and neither do you.”
“I hope it helps you, but the study is designed to answer a question, not to tailor a plan just for you.”


Does this sound like care, research, or both?
What would you still want to know before saying yes?
If your doctor is the one recruiting you, what confusion is most likely?











Note



Three starting points matter here:

Research can happen inside ordinary care settings without becoming ordinary care. Same clinic, different goal.
Randomization is the clue. Once assignment is by chance, the clinician is no longer choosing only on the basis of what seems best for this patient.
Hope is not the same as a promise. Ethical recruitment does not kill hope, but it cannot quietly market the study as personalized treatment.




The opening move should make students feel how easily research can sound like an ordinary treatment recommendation. The point is not to make them anti-research; it is to make them notice the difference in purpose.





By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Distinguish clinical care from clinical research, and say why the difference changes consent and oversight
Explain randomization, control, placebo, and blinding by walking through the 1954 polio trial
Tell a clinical endpoint from a surrogate, and judge whether a result could be chance
Summarize what trial phases I–IV are for
Explain the therapeutic misconception and why it matters at the bedside
Use the opioid-evidence story to say what a given study can and cannot support

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
You have metastatic cancer. Your oncologist says:

“The standard drug has stopped working.”
“There is a study you could join, but I need to be clear: it is a randomized trial.”
“One group gets the current standard treatment. The other gets the new drug.”
“I do not get to choose your group, and neither do you.”
“I hope it helps you, but the study is designed to answer a question, not to tailor a plan just for you.”


Does this sound like care, research, or both?
What would you still want to know before saying yes?
If your doctor is the one recruiting you, what confusion is most likely?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Note



Three starting points matter here:

Research can happen inside ordinary care settings without becoming ordinary care. Same clinic, different goal.
Randomization is the clue. Once assignment is by chance, the clinician is no longer choosing only on the basis of what seems best for this patient.
Hope is not the same as a promise. Ethical recruitment does not kill hope, but it cannot quietly market the study as personalized treatment.




The opening move should make students feel how easily research can sound like an ordinary treatment recommendation. The point is not to make them anti-research; it is to make them notice the difference in purpose.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Distinguish clinical care from clinical research, and say why the difference changes consent and oversight
Explain randomization, control, placebo, and blinding by walking through the 1954 polio trial
Tell a clinical endpoint from a surrogate, and judge whether a result could be chance
Summarize what trial phases I–IV are for
Explain the therapeutic misconception and why it matters at the bedside
Use the opioid-evidence story to say what a given study can and cannot support

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
2.1 One Crucial Difference

Clinical care aims to help the patient in front of you
Clinical research aims to produce generalizable knowledge (Emanuel et al. 2000)
Both may benefit a patient
But direct patient benefit is not what makes an activity research
That difference changes consent, oversight, and acceptable risk

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 2.2 The Same Room Can Hide the Difference
text:
2.2 The Same Room Can Hide the Difference


Why it feels like care

Same hospital
Same white coats
Same lab draws and imaging
Same reassuring language of “options”
Often the same physician


Why it is not the same

The protocol fixes what can happen
Randomization may assign the arm
The main goal is learning, not tailoring
The results are meant to guide future care
Independent review is required before it begins

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
2.3 Why Research Exists

Without research, medicine would freeze in place
New drugs, devices, and procedures need to be tested before they become standard
Even old treatments need comparison, follow-up, and correction
Ethical research is not the enemy of patient care
Ethical research is one reason patient care can improve at all

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 2.4 Research vs. Clinical Care at a Glance
text:
2.4 Research vs. Clinical Care at a Glance








Question
Clinical care
Clinical research




Main goal
Help this patient now
Produce reliable knowledge


Who should benefit first?
The individual patient
Future patients, and sometimes current participants


Who sets the plan?
Clinician and patient
Protocol, eligibility rules, and study design


What governs it?
Professional standards, licensure, ordinary consent
IRB review, protocol rules, research consent (US Department of Health and Human Services 2018)


What counts as success?
Good care for this person
Valid evidence that answers the research question

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
2.5 The Physician-Investigator Tension

A clinician may also be a physician-investigator
That can be efficient and even helpful
It can also blur whether advice is being given as a doctor or as a researcher
Modern research ethics assumes this tension is real, not rare (World Medical Association 2024)
That is one reason independent review matters

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.1 Why Randomize?

If doctors chose the groups, healthier and sicker patients could cluster without anyone meaning to (National Cancer Institute 2024)
Randomization assigns people by chance
That helps keep the comparison scientifically fair
But it also means the clinician is no longer hand-picking the treatment for this patient
So randomization is ethically important, not just statistically useful

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 3.2 When Is It OK to Randomize a Sick Person?
text:
3.2 When Is It OK to Randomize a Sick Person?

The honest answer needs a concept: clinical equipoise
It means the expert community is genuinely unsure which arm is better
If we already knew, randomizing would be wrong — we’d be assigning some patients to the worse option on purpose
Equipoise is the moral license for a trial
This is why “I’d just give everyone the new drug” misses the point: we do not yet know it is better

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 3.3 One Map of a Parallel-Group Trial
text:
3.3 One Map of a Parallel-Group Trial







rct


A

People with the
condition



B

Eligibility check
and consent



A->B





C

Randomize



B->C





D

Arm 1
new treatment



C->D





E

Arm 2
standard care or
control



C->E





F

Follow-up
and outcome data



D->F





E->F





G

Compare results



F->G






 A plain-language map of a parallel-group randomized trial. The key ethical moment is the middle: once participants consent and are randomized, no one chooses the arm just for that patient’s benefit.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.4 A Real Trial: Polio, 1954

In 1952 the United States recorded roughly 58,000 polio cases, most of them children — it was the most feared childhood disease in the country.
Jonas Salk’s killed-virus injected vaccine looked promising, but “promising” vaccines had failed and even harmed children before.
So the country ran the largest medical experiment in history: about 1.8 million schoolchildren, the “Polio Pioneers,” organized by the March of Dimes and directed by Thomas Francis Jr.
The trial’s job was not to deliver a cure but to prove whether there was one.
Every mechanic from the last few slides — randomize, control, blind, compare — was used on these children, and the design choices decided whether anyone could believe the result (Francis et al. 1955).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.5 Two Designs, One Trial
The 1954 trial actually ran two designs at once — and that is the lesson:









Placebo-controlled areas
Observed-control areas




Who got vaccine
Grades 1–3 randomized to vaccine or saline
Only 2nd-grade volunteers vaccinated


The controls
Randomized peers given a saline shot
Grades 1 and 3, simply observed


Blinding
Double-blind — no one knew who got what
Not blinded


How trustworthy
High — groups comparable, judgment unbiased
Weaker — volunteers differ; diagnosis can be swayed



Same disease, same year, same vaccine: the design, not the vaccine, is what made one half of the trial believable.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.6 What the Control Group Gets

Sometimes the control group gets current standard care
Sometimes it gets standard care plus a placebo
Sometimes, if no proven treatment exists, it may get placebo or no active treatment (National Cancer Institute 2024; World Medical Association 2024)
Control does not mean “ignored”
Everyone in the study is still monitored under a protocol

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.7 Placebo Does Not Always Mean “Nothing”

A placebo can do different jobs.

If no proven treatment exists, placebo may stand in for no active treatment.
If a proven treatment already exists, placebo is often added on top of standard care so no one is denied basic treatment.
The ethical question is not just “placebo or no placebo?” It is what participants are missing by being in that arm (National Cancer Institute 2024; World Medical Association 2024).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.8 What Blinding Actually Does

Blinding means some people in the trial do not know which arm a participant is in
Single-blind usually means the participant does not know
Double-blind usually means the participant and the treating team or evaluators do not know
The point is to reduce expectation effects and biased judgment
“Double-blind” is about cleaner evidence, not secrecy for its own sake

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.9 Why Blinding Mattered in 1954

Diagnosing mild polio is partly a judgment call — a borderline weak limb can be read either way. In the 1954 trial’s observed-control areas, the examining doctors knew which children had been vaccinated. A doctor who hoped the vaccine worked could, without any dishonesty, lean toward “not polio” in a vaccinated child.
That is exactly why the placebo-controlled areas were double-blind: no one at the bedside knew, so hope could not tip the count.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 3.10 Who Watches the Trial While It Runs?
text:
3.10 Who Watches the Trial While It Runs?

A trial is not left alone until it ends
An independent data safety monitoring board (DSMB) reviews the data as it accumulates
It can stop a trial early for harm — one arm is clearly hurting people
It can also stop early for benefit — one arm is so clearly better that continuing the other is no longer fair
Equipoise has a deadline: once the evidence is in, the trial must end

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.11 The Same Machinery, Today

The 2020 COVID-19 vaccine trials used exactly the 1954 logic at modern scale: tens of thousands of volunteers, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled.
The primary endpoint was a clinical one — laboratory-confirmed symptomatic COVID-19 — not just an antibody level in the blood.
An independent data safety monitoring board reviewed the accumulating results at planned interim points.
When the effect was large and the safety data adequate, the FDA granted Emergency Use Authorization, with full approval following after more follow-up.
Seventy years apart, the polio and COVID trials are recognizably the same instrument, used carefully because the stakes were enormous.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 4.1 What Are We Even Measuring? Endpoints
text:
4.1 What Are We Even Measuring? Endpoints

A clinical endpoint is something a patient can feel or would obviously care about: living longer, avoiding a stroke, walking without pain.
A surrogate endpoint is a stand-in measured because it is faster or cheaper — a lab number, a scan finding, a blood pressure reading.
Surrogates speed research up, but a treatment can move the surrogate and still not help patients — or even harm them.
The classic warning is the CAST trial: drugs that “fixed” an abnormal heart rhythm on the monitor actually increased deaths.
So the first question to ask of any trial is: did patients actually do better, or did a number just move?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.2 Could It Just Be Chance?

Any two groups of people differ a little by luck, even if the treatment does nothing at all.
Statistics ask one question: how likely is a difference this big if the treatment really had no effect?
“Statistically significant” roughly means “probably not just luck” — it does not mean large, important, or certain.
Small trials are easily fooled by chance, which is why large samples and independent replication matter so much.
A single significant result is a lead worth following, not a verdict to act on alone.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.3 Honest Trials Can Still Mislead

A result can mislead even when no one lied or faked anything.
Who got into the trial limits who the answer applies to: narrow eligibility rules can exclude the very patients you treat.
People who drop out are usually different from those who finish, so analyzing only the “completers” quietly flatters the treatment.
Intention-to-treat analysis — count everyone in the group they were randomized to, even if they quit — is the standard guard against that.
The disciplined question is always: who is missing from this result, and which way would that bias it?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.4 When a Letter Becomes “Evidence”

In 1980 a five-sentence letter to the editor reported that, among hospitalized patients given opioids, addiction was rare (Porter and Jick 1980).
It was not a trial: no randomization, no control, no long-term or outpatient follow-up.
Over the next two decades it was cited hundreds of times as if it had proven that long-term outpatient opioids rarely cause addiction.
The reading-evidence lesson is exact: the type of study and who was in it cap what any result can support.
How that misreading was promoted is a research-integrity story we return to later — here the point is simply that a letter is not an RCT.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.5 Thought Question

A new drug’s Phase III trial is reported as “positive.”

It significantly improved a surrogate marker on a scan.
The trial was small, and about 25% of participants dropped out.
The analysis included only those who completed.


Name the three weaknesses you can already see.
What would you want to know before telling a patient this drug “works”?
Is “the FDA might approve it” the same as “it helps patients”?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.6 Make It Contemporary

Regulators sometimes grant accelerated approval based on a surrogate endpoint, with a confirmatory trial promised later.

Patients with no other option get the drug years sooner.
Some confirmatory trials later show no real benefit — and a few are never finished.

Is faster access worth weaker evidence? Who should decide, and what should happen if the confirmatory trial fails?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.7 After You’ve Discussed






Note



Three distinctions to carry forward:

Statistically significant ≠ clinically meaningful. A real but tiny effect on a surrogate can be true and still not worth having.
Regulatory approval ≠ proven patient benefit. Approval is a decision under uncertainty, not a guarantee.
The standing questions are always the same: what was measured, in whom, and who is missing from the result?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
5.1 Four Phases, Four Questions








Phase
The main question
Typical scale




I
Is it safe? What dose can people tolerate?
Small


II
Does it seem to work?
Larger


III
Is it better than the current standard?
Large, often randomized


IV
What happens after approval in the real world?
Very large, longer-term

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
5.2 One Simple Phase Map







phases


A

Phase I
small group
safety and dose



B

Phase II
more participants
early evidence of effect



A->B





C

Phase III
large comparison
against standard care



B->C





D

Phase IV
after approval
long-term follow-up



C->D






 A plain-language phase map. The main ethical point is that ‘clinical trial’ covers activities with very different goals, risks, and likely benefits.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 5.3 What Each Phase Is Really Asking
text:
5.3 What Each Phase Is Really Asking

Phase I is small (often 15–30 people in cancer) and asks what dose is tolerable and what harms appear; direct benefit is not the point, which is what makes it ethically hard (National Cancer Institute 2024).
Phase II enrolls more people and asks whether the treatment seems to work, while safety is still watched closely; many promising drugs fail right here.
Phase III is the large, usually randomized comparison against the best existing option, and it is where placebo and control debates bite hardest because its result becomes the new standard.
Phase IV runs after approval and catches rare harms and real-world performance that tightly controlled trials miss.
“Clinical trial” is therefore not one thing — the same words cover wildly different risks, hopes, and likely benefits.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
5.4 Why Phases Matter Ethically

Students often hear “clinical trial” and imagine one thing
But Phase I and Phase IV do not ask the same question or offer the same likely benefits
Consent should match the phase, not rely on a generic script
Risks, hopes, and expectations shift from phase to phase
So the ethical explanation has to be specific

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.1 The Therapeutic Misconception

Therapeutic misconception happens when participants think the study intervention is being chosen mainly for their personal medical benefit (Appelbaum et al. 1982)
The misunderstanding is not just “I hope this helps”
It is more like “my doctor is selecting this because it is best for me”
That is often false once protocol rules and randomization control the options
A person can sign a form and still misunderstand the basic situation

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.2 Why It Happens So Easily

Recruitment often comes from a trusted clinician
Serious illness makes hope urgent
Research visits look like treatment visits
Words like “option” and “opportunity” blur categories
Many people hear “new” and assume “better”

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 6.3 A Research Consent Is Not a Treatment Consent
text:
6.3 A Research Consent Is Not a Treatment Consent

Valid consent has four elements; research adds requirements on top of them
It must say, in plain words, “this is research” — not treatment chosen for you
It must disclose randomization: a chance process, not your clinician, may pick your arm
It must state the goal is knowledge, and that direct benefit is uncertain or unlikely
It must make withdrawal at any time, without penalty, explicit
The nurse is often the one who notices a form was signed but not understood

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.4 The Dangerous Sentence


“This study may help you” can be true. “This study is being chosen because it is the best treatment for you” is often false.


The distinction is what the therapeutic misconception tries to protect (Appelbaum et al. 1982; Emanuel et al. 2000).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 6.5 Early-Phase Trials and Thin Personal Benefit
text:
6.5 Early-Phase Trials and Thin Personal Benefit

Healthy volunteers and desperate patients join studies for very different reasons
In Phase I, researchers mainly ask what dose is tolerable and what harms appear
Some participants may benefit, but many will not
Payment, hope, and lack of alternatives can all cloud judgment
Ethical recruitment has to say this plainly

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.6 Thought Question

You are a research nurse enrolling patients in a first-in-human oncology trial.

A patient says, “If you are offering this, you must think it gives me the best chance.”
The oncologist who is also the principal investigator is standing beside you.


What is the most important misunderstanding to correct?
How blunt should you be about the low chance of direct benefit?
If the patient says, “I know the odds are low, but I want to try anyway,” has the misconception been fixed?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.7 Make It Contemporary

The same structure appears outside oncology too:

A family enrolls a child in a gene-therapy study because no approved treatment exists.
A healthy adult joins a Phase I trial mainly because the payment covers two months of rent.

Both may be voluntary. But are both equally informed and equally free?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.8 After You’ve Discussed






Note



Three payoffs to insist on:

Hope is allowed. The goal is not to turn participants cynical; it is to keep hope from masquerading as evidence.
Misunderstanding is not the same as irrationality. A person may fully understand that a study is not personalized care and still choose it.
The hardest part is relational. When a trusted clinician recruits for a study, the burden to separate care from research falls more heavily on the professional, not less (World Medical Association 2024).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
7.1 The Main Distinction

Trials are ethically hard because they borrow the setting of care but not its single-minded goal
Randomization, blinding, control groups, and phases are scientific tools
The same tools also let you read a result: what was measured, could it be chance, who is missing
Those tools are exactly why research needs its own ethics
Part B turns from the structure of trials to the rulebook meant to protect the people inside them

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
7.2 Wrapping Up Part A

Clinical research is not ordinary care with extra paperwork. Its purpose is to produce reliable knowledge, which is why it uses randomization, control groups, blinding, and staged phases — visible in one real instrument, the 1954 polio trial, and again in the COVID-19 vaccine trials. Those same tools also teach you to read a study: clinical versus surrogate endpoints, whether a result could be chance, and who dropped out or was left out. The key task in Part A is learning to say, clearly and without panic: this is research, not just treatment by another name — and here is how good its evidence actually is.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
7.3 References


Appelbaum, Paul S., Loren H. Roth, and Charles Lidz. 1982. “The Therapeutic Misconception: Informed Consent in Psychiatric Research.” International Journal of Law and Psychiatry 5 (3-4): 319–29. https://doi.org/10.1016/0160-2527(82)90026-7.


Emanuel, Ezekiel J., David Wendler, and Christine Grady. 2000. “What Makes Clinical Research Ethical?” JAMA 283 (20): 2701–11. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.283.20.2701.


Francis, Thomas et al. 1955. “An Evaluation of the 1954 Poliomyelitis Vaccine Trials: Summary Report.” American Journal of Public Health 45 (5): 1–63.


National Cancer Institute. 2024. How Do Clinical Trials Work? National Cancer Institute. https://www.cancer.gov/research/participate/clinical-trials/how-trials-work.


Porter, Jane, and Hershel Jick. 1980. “Addiction Rare in Patients Treated with Narcotics.” New England Journal of Medicine 302 (2): 123. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJM198001103020221.


US Department of Health and Human Services. 2018. Federal Policy for the Protection of Human Subjects (“the Common Rule”). Code of Federal Regulations, Title 45, Part 46. https://www.hhs.gov/ohrp/regulations-and-policy/regulations/common-rule/index.html.


World Medical Association. 2024. WMA Declaration of Helsinki – Ethical Principles for Medical Research Involving Human Participants. Adopted by the 75th WMA General Assembly, Helsinki, Finland. https://www.wma.net/policies-post/wma-declaration-of-helsinki/.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: A Trial Offer at the Worst Time
text:
A Trial Offer at the Worst Time

You have metastatic cancer. Your oncologist says:

“The standard drug has stopped working.”
“There is a study you could join, but I need to be clear: it is a randomized trial.”
“One group gets the current standard treatment. The other gets the new drug.”
“I do not get to choose your group, and neither do you.”
“I hope it helps you, but the study is designed to answer a question, not to tailor a plan just for you.”


Does this sound like care, research, or both?
What would you still want to know before saying yes?
If your doctor is the one recruiting you, what confusion is most likely?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Three starting points matter here:

Research can happen inside ordinary care settings without becoming ordinary care. Same clinic, different goal.
Randomization is the clue. Once assignment is by chance, the clinician is no longer choosing only on the basis of what seems best for this patient.
Hope is not the same as a promise. Ethical recruitment does not kill hope, but it cannot quietly market the study as personalized treatment.





The opening move should make students feel how easily research can sound like an ordinary treatment recommendation. The point is not to make them anti-research; it is to make them notice the difference in purpose.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Today

By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Distinguish clinical care from clinical research, and say why the difference changes consent and oversight
Explain randomization, control, placebo, and blinding by walking through the 1954 polio trial
Tell a clinical endpoint from a surrogate, and judge whether a result could be chance
Summarize what trial phases I–IV are for
Explain the therapeutic misconception and why it matters at the bedside
Use the opioid-evidence story to say what a given study can and cannot support

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
One Crucial Difference

Clinical care aims to help the patient in front of you
Clinical research aims to produce generalizable knowledge (Emanuel et al. 2000)
Both may benefit a patient
But direct patient benefit is not what makes an activity research
That difference changes consent, oversight, and acceptable risk

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: The Same Room Can Hide the Difference
text:
The Same Room Can Hide the Difference


Why it feels like care

Same hospital
Same white coats
Same lab draws and imaging
Same reassuring language of “options”
Often the same physician


Why it is not the same

The protocol fixes what can happen
Randomization may assign the arm
The main goal is learning, not tailoring
The results are meant to guide future care
Independent review is required before it begins

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Why Research Exists

Without research, medicine would freeze in place
New drugs, devices, and procedures need to be tested before they become standard
Even old treatments need comparison, follow-up, and correction
Ethical research is not the enemy of patient care
Ethical research is one reason patient care can improve at all

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: Research vs. Clinical Care at a Glance
text:
Research vs. Clinical Care at a Glance








Question
Clinical care
Clinical research




Main goal
Help this patient now
Produce reliable knowledge


Who should benefit first?
The individual patient
Future patients, and sometimes current participants


Who sets the plan?
Clinician and patient
Protocol, eligibility rules, and study design


What governs it?
Professional standards, licensure, ordinary consent
IRB review, protocol rules, research consent (US Department of Health and Human Services 2018)


What counts as success?
Good care for this person
Valid evidence that answers the research question

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Physician-Investigator Tension

A clinician may also be a physician-investigator
That can be efficient and even helpful
It can also blur whether advice is being given as a doctor or as a researcher
Modern research ethics assumes this tension is real, not rare (World Medical Association 2024)
That is one reason independent review matters

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Why Randomize?

If doctors chose the groups, healthier and sicker patients could cluster without anyone meaning to (National Cancer Institute 2024)
Randomization assigns people by chance
That helps keep the comparison scientifically fair
But it also means the clinician is no longer hand-picking the treatment for this patient
So randomization is ethically important, not just statistically useful

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: When Is It OK to Randomize a Sick Person?
text:
When Is It OK to Randomize a Sick Person?

The honest answer needs a concept: clinical equipoise
It means the expert community is genuinely unsure which arm is better
If we already knew, randomizing would be wrong — we’d be assigning some patients to the worse option on purpose
Equipoise is the moral license for a trial
This is why “I’d just give everyone the new drug” misses the point: we do not yet know it is better

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: One Map of a Parallel-Group Trial
text:
One Map of a Parallel-Group Trial







rct


A

People with the
condition



B

Eligibility check
and consent



A->B





C

Randomize



B->C





D

Arm 1
new treatment



C->D





E

Arm 2
standard care or
control



C->E





F

Follow-up
and outcome data



D->F





E->F





G

F->G






 A plain-language map of a parallel-group randomized trial. The key ethical moment is the middle: once participants consent and are randomized, no one chooses the arm just for that patient’s benefit.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
A Real Trial: Polio, 1954

In 1952 the United States recorded roughly 58,000 polio cases, most of them children — it was the most feared childhood disease in the country.
Jonas Salk’s killed-virus injected vaccine looked promising, but “promising” vaccines had failed and even harmed children before.
So the country ran the largest medical experiment in history: about 1.8 million schoolchildren, the “Polio Pioneers,” organized by the March of Dimes and directed by Thomas Francis Jr.
The trial’s job was not to deliver a cure but to prove whether there was one.
Every mechanic from the last few slides — randomize, control, blind, compare — was used on these children, and the design choices decided whether anyone could believe the result (Francis et al. 1955).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Two Designs, One Trial
The 1954 trial actually ran two designs at once — and that is the lesson:









Placebo-controlled areas
Observed-control areas




Who got vaccine
Grades 1–3 randomized to vaccine or saline
Only 2nd-grade volunteers vaccinated


The controls
Randomized peers given a saline shot
Grades 1 and 3, simply observed


Blinding
Double-blind — no one knew who got what
Not blinded


How trustworthy
High — groups comparable, judgment unbiased
Weaker — volunteers differ; diagnosis can be swayed



Same disease, same year, same vaccine: the design, not the vaccine, is what made one half of the trial believable.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
What the Control Group Gets

Sometimes the control group gets current standard care
Sometimes it gets standard care plus a placebo
Sometimes, if no proven treatment exists, it may get placebo or no active treatment (National Cancer Institute 2024; World Medical Association 2024)
Control does not mean “ignored”
Everyone in the study is still monitored under a protocol

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Placebo Does Not Always Mean “Nothing”

A placebo can do different jobs.

If no proven treatment exists, placebo may stand in for no active treatment.
If a proven treatment already exists, placebo is often added on top of standard care so no one is denied basic treatment.
The ethical question is not just “placebo or no placebo?” It is what participants are missing by being in that arm (National Cancer Institute 2024; World Medical Association 2024).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
What Blinding Actually Does

Blinding means some people in the trial do not know which arm a participant is in
Single-blind usually means the participant does not know
Double-blind usually means the participant and the treating team or evaluators do not know
The point is to reduce expectation effects and biased judgment
“Double-blind” is about cleaner evidence, not secrecy for its own sake

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Why Blinding Mattered in 1954

Diagnosing mild polio is partly a judgment call — a borderline weak limb can be read either way. In the 1954 trial’s observed-control areas, the examining doctors knew which children had been vaccinated. A doctor who hoped the vaccine worked could, without any dishonesty, lean toward “not polio” in a vaccinated child.
That is exactly why the placebo-controlled areas were double-blind: no one at the bedside knew, so hope could not tip the count.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: Who Watches the Trial While It Runs?
text:
Who Watches the Trial While It Runs?

A trial is not left alone until it ends
An independent data safety monitoring board (DSMB) reviews the data as it accumulates
It can stop a trial early for harm — one arm is clearly hurting people
It can also stop early for benefit — one arm is so clearly better that continuing the other is no longer fair
Equipoise has a deadline: once the evidence is in, the trial must end

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Same Machinery, Today

The 2020 COVID-19 vaccine trials used exactly the 1954 logic at modern scale: tens of thousands of volunteers, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled.
The primary endpoint was a clinical one — laboratory-confirmed symptomatic COVID-19 — not just an antibody level in the blood.
An independent data safety monitoring board reviewed the accumulating results at planned interim points.
When the effect was large and the safety data adequate, the FDA granted Emergency Use Authorization, with full approval following after more follow-up.
Seventy years apart, the polio and COVID trials are recognizably the same instrument, used carefully because the stakes were enormous.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: What Are We Even Measuring? Endpoints
text:
What Are We Even Measuring? Endpoints

A clinical endpoint is something a patient can feel or would obviously care about: living longer, avoiding a stroke, walking without pain.
A surrogate endpoint is a stand-in measured because it is faster or cheaper — a lab number, a scan finding, a blood pressure reading.
Surrogates speed research up, but a treatment can move the surrogate and still not help patients — or even harm them.
The classic warning is the CAST trial: drugs that “fixed” an abnormal heart rhythm on the monitor actually increased deaths.
So the first question to ask of any trial is: did patients actually do better, or did a number just move?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Could It Just Be Chance?

Any two groups of people differ a little by luck, even if the treatment does nothing at all.
Statistics ask one question: how likely is a difference this big if the treatment really had no effect?
“Statistically significant” roughly means “probably not just luck” — it does not mean large, important, or certain.
Small trials are easily fooled by chance, which is why large samples and independent replication matter so much.
A single significant result is a lead worth following, not a verdict to act on alone.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Honest Trials Can Still Mislead

A result can mislead even when no one lied or faked anything.
Who got into the trial limits who the answer applies to: narrow eligibility rules can exclude the very patients you treat.
People who drop out are usually different from those who finish, so analyzing only the “completers” quietly flatters the treatment.
Intention-to-treat analysis — count everyone in the group they were randomized to, even if they quit — is the standard guard against that.
The disciplined question is always: who is missing from this result, and which way would that bias it?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
When a Letter Becomes “Evidence”

In 1980 a five-sentence letter to the editor reported that, among hospitalized patients given opioids, addiction was rare (Porter and Jick 1980).
It was not a trial: no randomization, no control, no long-term or outpatient follow-up.
Over the next two decades it was cited hundreds of times as if it had proven that long-term outpatient opioids rarely cause addiction.
The reading-evidence lesson is exact: the type of study and who was in it cap what any result can support.
How that misreading was promoted is a research-integrity story we return to later — here the point is simply that a letter is not an RCT.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Thought Question

A new drug’s Phase III trial is reported as “positive.”

It significantly improved a surrogate marker on a scan.
The trial was small, and about 25% of participants dropped out.
The analysis included only those who completed.


Name the three weaknesses you can already see.
What would you want to know before telling a patient this drug “works”?
Is “the FDA might approve it” the same as “it helps patients”?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Make It Contemporary

Regulators sometimes grant accelerated approval based on a surrogate endpoint, with a confirmatory trial promised later.

Patients with no other option get the drug years sooner.
Some confirmatory trials later show no real benefit — and a few are never finished.

Is faster access worth weaker evidence? Who should decide, and what should happen if the confirmatory trial fails?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Three distinctions to carry forward:

Statistically significant ≠ clinically meaningful. A real but tiny effect on a surrogate can be true and still not worth having.
Regulatory approval ≠ proven patient benefit. Approval is a decision under uncertainty, not a guarantee.
The standing questions are always the same: what was measured, in whom, and who is missing from the result?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Four Phases, Four Questions








Phase
The main question
Typical scale




I
Is it safe? What dose can people tolerate?
Small


II
Does it seem to work?
Larger


III
Is it better than the current standard?
Large, often randomized


IV
What happens after approval in the real world?
Very large, longer-term

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
One Simple Phase Map







phases


A

Phase I
small group
safety and dose



B

Phase II
more participants
early evidence of effect



A->B





C

Phase III
large comparison
against standard care



B->C





D

Phase IV
after approval
long-term follow-up



C->D






 A plain-language phase map. The main ethical point is that ‘clinical trial’ covers activities with very different goals, risks, and likely benefits.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: What Each Phase Is Really Asking
text:
What Each Phase Is Really Asking

Phase I is small (often 15–30 people in cancer) and asks what dose is tolerable and what harms appear; direct benefit is not the point, which is what makes it ethically hard (National Cancer Institute 2024).
Phase II enrolls more people and asks whether the treatment seems to work, while safety is still watched closely; many promising drugs fail right here.
Phase III is the large, usually randomized comparison against the best existing option, and it is where placebo and control debates bite hardest because its result becomes the new standard.
Phase IV runs after approval and catches rare harms and real-world performance that tightly controlled trials miss.
“Clinical trial” is therefore not one thing — the same words cover wildly different risks, hopes, and likely benefits.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Why Phases Matter Ethically

Students often hear “clinical trial” and imagine one thing
But Phase I and Phase IV do not ask the same question or offer the same likely benefits
Consent should match the phase, not rely on a generic script
Risks, hopes, and expectations shift from phase to phase
So the ethical explanation has to be specific

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Therapeutic Misconception

Therapeutic misconception happens when participants think the study intervention is being chosen mainly for their personal medical benefit (Appelbaum et al. 1982)
The misunderstanding is not just “I hope this helps”
It is more like “my doctor is selecting this because it is best for me”
That is often false once protocol rules and randomization control the options
A person can sign a form and still misunderstand the basic situation

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Why It Happens So Easily

Recruitment often comes from a trusted clinician
Serious illness makes hope urgent
Research visits look like treatment visits
Words like “option” and “opportunity” blur categories
Many people hear “new” and assume “better”

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: A Research Consent Is Not a Treatment Consent
text:
A Research Consent Is Not a Treatment Consent

Valid consent has four elements; research adds requirements on top of them
It must say, in plain words, “this is research” — not treatment chosen for you
It must disclose randomization: a chance process, not your clinician, may pick your arm
It must state the goal is knowledge, and that direct benefit is uncertain or unlikely
It must make withdrawal at any time, without penalty, explicit
The nurse is often the one who notices a form was signed but not understood

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Dangerous Sentence


“This study may help you” can be true. “This study is being chosen because it is the best treatment for you” is often false.


The distinction is what the therapeutic misconception tries to protect (Appelbaum et al. 1982; Emanuel et al. 2000).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: Early-Phase Trials and Thin Personal Benefit
text:
Early-Phase Trials and Thin Personal Benefit

Healthy volunteers and desperate patients join studies for very different reasons
In Phase I, researchers mainly ask what dose is tolerable and what harms appear
Some participants may benefit, but many will not
Payment, hope, and lack of alternatives can all cloud judgment
Ethical recruitment has to say this plainly

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Thought Question

You are a research nurse enrolling patients in a first-in-human oncology trial.

A patient says, “If you are offering this, you must think it gives me the best chance.”
The oncologist who is also the principal investigator is standing beside you.


What is the most important misunderstanding to correct?
How blunt should you be about the low chance of direct benefit?
If the patient says, “I know the odds are low, but I want to try anyway,” has the misconception been fixed?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Make It Contemporary

The same structure appears outside oncology too:

A family enrolls a child in a gene-therapy study because no approved treatment exists.
A healthy adult joins a Phase I trial mainly because the payment covers two months of rent.

Both may be voluntary. But are both equally informed and equally free?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Three payoffs to insist on:

Hope is allowed. The goal is not to turn participants cynical; it is to keep hope from masquerading as evidence.
Misunderstanding is not the same as irrationality. A person may fully understand that a study is not personalized care and still choose it.
The hardest part is relational. When a trusted clinician recruits for a study, the burden to separate care from research falls more heavily on the professional, not less (World Medical Association 2024).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Main Distinction

Trials are ethically hard because they borrow the setting of care but not its single-minded goal
Randomization, blinding, control groups, and phases are scientific tools
The same tools also let you read a result: what was measured, could it be chance, who is missing
Those tools are exactly why research needs its own ethics
Part B turns from the structure of trials to the rulebook meant to protect the people inside them

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Wrapping Up Part A

Clinical research is not ordinary care with extra paperwork. Its purpose is to produce reliable knowledge, which is why it uses randomization, control groups, blinding, and staged phases — visible in one real instrument, the 1954 polio trial, and again in the COVID-19 vaccine trials. Those same tools also teach you to read a study: clinical versus surrogate endpoints, whether a result could be chance, and who dropped out or was left out. The key task in Part A is learning to say, clearly and without panic: this is research, not just treatment by another name — and here is how good its evidence actually is.
