title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Refusal Is the Real Test

Easy cases are consent cases: the patient wants what is offered, and everyone relaxes.
The hard cases are refusals, where a competent person says no to something that would clearly help.
An informed refusal demands the same four elements as an informed consent — disclosure, understanding, voluntariness, capacity.
The landmark refusals are concrete: Jehovah’s Witness patients declining life-saving transfusion, and Bouvia v. Superior Court (1986), where a woman with cerebral palsy won the right to refuse force-feeding (California Court of Appeal 1986).
The course’s hardest refusal case, and your paired case study, is Dax Cowart.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Dax Cowart

In 1973 a propane explosion killed Dax Cowart’s father and left Dax, then 25, severely burned and blinded.
Throughout roughly fourteen months of agonizing treatment he repeatedly and lucidly demanded that it stop; clinicians treated him anyway.
He went on to earn a law degree, marry, and become a patient-rights advocate; his case is preserved in the films Please Let Me Die (1974) and Dax’s Case (1985).
Until his death in 2019 he held a deliberately uncomfortable position: he was glad to be alive and insisted his autonomy had been gravely violated.
That paradox — a good outcome reached by a wrong route — is exactly why the case refuses to resolve neatly (Cowart and Burt 1998).


This is the paired case study. Students don’t need the full facts now — only enough to feel the autonomy/beneficence collision before they read it.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Thought Question

A competent adult refuses a blood transfusion that would almost certainly save their life.

Is honoring the refusal respect — or abandonment?
What is the clinician still obligated to keep doing after the refusal?
Mrs. R, in tears, says “I can’t even think about this right now.” Is that a refusal, indecision, or lost capacity — and how would you tell?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Make It Contemporary

Change one fact: the refusal isn’t religious. The patient read online that transfusions are dangerous and won’t budge.

Same legal right. Does the reason change what you owe them?
Does correcting the misinformation respect her autonomy — or undermine it?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note



Respecting a decision is not endorsing the reasoning. You can believe she is wrong and still be bound by her refusal.
The capacity bar is applied unevenly. We rarely test capacity when patients say yes; we scrutinize it when they say no. If every risky refusal counted as incapacity, informed consent collapses back into paternalism. The honest question is what danger is enough to re-assess — not to override.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Why Confidentiality Matters

Confidentiality is one of medicine’s oldest promises — it is in the Hippocratic Oath, long before it was in any statute.
Its value is partly instrumental: patients only disclose stigmatized, decision-critical facts (drug use, sexual history, suicidal thoughts) if they trust the information will be held.
Its value is also intrinsic: control over who knows your medical story is part of being treated as a person, not a case.
The evidence is concrete — when adolescents doubt confidentiality, they measurably withhold information and forgo care.
So confidentiality is ethical before it is legal, and breaking it quietly damages care even when no one finds out.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: HIPAA Is Not a Gag Order
text:
HIPAA Is Not a Gag Order

HIPAA is the U.S. federal health-privacy rule, and it is one of the most misunderstood laws in medicine (U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Civil Rights 2025).
It explicitly permits sharing for treatment, payment, and operations, and to avert a serious and imminent threat — it is not a vow of silence.
It runs on a “minimum necessary” principle: access and share only what the task in front of you actually requires.
Misreading it has real costs: families are stonewalled and care coordination suffers because staff wrongly believe HIPAA forbids all disclosure.
Concretely, if Mrs. R’s sister calls for an update, you generally cannot even confirm she is a patient without Mrs. R’s authorization — and that is the rule working as intended.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
HHS on the Privacy Rule


“A major goal of the Privacy Rule is to assure that individuals’ health information is properly protected while allowing the flow of health information needed to provide and promote high quality health care and to protect the public’s health and well being. The Rule strikes a balance that permits important uses of information, while protecting the privacy of people who seek care and healing.”


U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Summary of the HIPAA Privacy Rule (U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Civil Rights 2025)

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
May Disclose vs. Must Disclose


You may disclose when

The patient authorizes it
It is necessary for treatment
Law permits limited operational use
A serious threat may justify a warning
Legal process requires a response


You must disclose

Suspected child abuse or neglect
Certain infectious diseases
Some court-ordered disclosures
Some state-mandated reporting
(The exact list varies by state)

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Confidentiality: Default and Exceptions







conf


A

Protected health
information



B

Default:
keep private



A->B





C

Patient authorizes
sharing



B->C


exception



D

Law requires
reporting



B->D


exception



E

Serious threat
may justify warning



B->E


exception




 Confidentiality starts from privacy, not disclosure. The legal and ethical question is which exceptions are strong enough to override that default.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: The Chart Is Now a Letter to the Patient
text:
The Chart Is Now a Letter to the Patient

Since 2021, the 21st Century Cures Act’s information-blocking rule gives patients near-immediate electronic access to their notes and most results.
“Open notes” is now the default: the patient will very likely read exactly what you wrote, often before you discuss it.
The upside is real — transparency improves trust, recall, and error-catching, and patients report feeling more in control.
The hard cases are also real: how to document a suspected abuse, a stigmatizing differential, or “patient appears to be a poor historian,” knowing the patient will see it.
The emerging norm is to write the chart as if it were addressed to the patient, because now it effectively is.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Tarasoff: Two Rulings, One Lesson

Prosenjit Poddar, a UC Berkeley student, told campus psychologist Lawrence Moore he intended to kill Tatiana Tarasoff after she rejected him.
Moore alerted campus police, who briefly detained Poddar and released him; no one warned Tatiana or her family, and Poddar killed her in 1969.
The California Supreme Court ruled twice: Tarasoff I (1974) created a duty to warn, and Tarasoff II (1976) broadened it to a duty to protect (Supreme Court of California 1976).
The famous dissent argued the rule would chill therapy and deter the very patients who most need help from speaking freely.
That tradeoff was never settled — it was handed to fifty state legislatures, which is why the duty looks different depending on where you practice.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Tarasoff I vs. Tarasoff II









Tarasoff I (1974)
Tarasoff II (1976)




Duty
Duty to warn the victim
Duty to protect the victim


Options
Tell the identifiable target
Warn, notify police, hospitalize — whatever a reasonable clinician would do


Trigger
A serious threat to an identifiable person
Same — but the response is broader and clinically judged


Today
Some states still use a narrow “warn” rule
Many adopted “protect”; some make it permissive, a few reject it entirely



The exam point: there is no single national Tarasoff rule — only a default ethical question (serious, specific danger to an identifiable person) answered differently by each state.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
In the Court’s Words


“We conclude that the public policy favoring protection of the confidential character of patient-psychotherapist communications must yield to the extent to which disclosure is essential to avert danger to others. The protective privilege ends where the public peril begins.”


Tarasoff v. Regents of the University of California (1976) (Supreme Court of California 1976)

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: The Case Nobody Warns You About
text:
The Case Nobody Warns You About

You are a nursing student on a med-surg unit. Your old roommate is admitted two doors down — and you have chart access.
You’re genuinely worried about her. You open her chart “just to see if she’s okay.” You tell no one. No one is harmed.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Snooping Is Not Hypothetical

Every chart access is logged; modern systems run automated audits that flag a record opened by someone not on the care team.
After staff repeatedly accessed celebrities’ records, UCLA’s health system fired employees, faced a federal settlement, and one worker was criminally convicted.
“I only looked, I didn’t tell anyone” is not a defense — the unauthorized access is itself the violation.
For a clinician, the realistic consequences stack: termination, loss of licensure, HIPAA penalties, and in some cases prosecution.
The trigger is almost always personal curiosity about someone the snooper knows or recognizes — exactly the roommate scenario.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Thought Question

Start with the roommate’s chart, then turn the privacy default on its head:

You looked “just to check she’s okay.” What exactly is the wrong, if no one ever finds out?
Does it change if she were your patient? If she’d want you to know? If you tell no one?
Now a patient tells you: “Sometimes I think about killing my ex — I know where she works.” What would you need to know before warning anyone?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Make It Contemporary


Hospitals run automated access audits; every year nurses are fired for snooping on relatives, exes, and celebrities
A patient posts a specific threat on Instagram, not in your office

Does “do I need this to care for this patient?” still draw the line in the digital version — both for the snooping and for the threat?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note



Access is not authorization. Being able to open a chart is not permission. The test is “do I need this to care for this patient?” Curiosity, worry, and good intentions are not exceptions.
The default flips, but the question doesn’t. Snooping over-discloses; Tarasoff under-discloses. Both are answered the same way — by asking what is genuinely necessary: to provide care, or to prevent serious, specific harm.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
How Truth-Telling Flipped







truth


A

1961 (Oken)
~90% of physicians did NOT
tell a cancer diagnosis



B

1979 (Novack)
~97% of physicians DID
tell the diagnosis



A->B


  one generation  




 In one human generation, the professional norm on disclosing a cancer diagnosis reversed almost completely. 



In 1961 Oken found that roughly nine in ten physicians withheld a cancer diagnosis to “protect” the patient; by 1979 Novack found the norm had almost exactly reversed (Oken 1961; Novack et al. 1979). The lesson is humbling: what felt like obvious kindness was a convention, not a moral fact — and conventions about disclosure still vary widely across cultures.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: Back to Mrs. R: “Please Don’t Tell Her”
text:
Back to Mrs. R: “Please Don’t Tell Her”

The biopsy is worse than expected. Before you go in, Dana stops you in the hallway:

“In our family, we don’t tell elders news like this. It will crush her. Please talk to me, not her.”

The team wants to respect the family’s culture. The team also wants to respect Mrs. R.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Thought Question


Whose decision is this — Mrs. R’s, Dana’s, or the team’s?
What is the first thing you should find out, and who do you ask?
What if you ask Mrs. R and she says, “I don’t want the details — talk to Dana”?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Make It Contemporary

A genetic test shows Mrs. R carries a mutation her adult siblings may also carry.

She does not want them told. They may be making medical decisions without information that could protect them.
Does her privacy outweigh their need to know? Is this the same problem as the family request, or a different one?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note



Only Mrs. R can waive her own right to know. A competent patient may autonomously choose not to receive information (“just talk to my daughter”) — that is respected autonomy. The family withholding it from her, unasked, is not their call.
So it isn’t “autonomy vs. culture.” The move is to ask Mrs. R — before the bad news — how much she wants to know and whom she wants involved. Culturally informed, and still hers.
The genetic case is different in kind: a third party’s welfare is now at stake. That is closer to Tarasoff than to truth-telling.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Mrs. R, Start to Finish

You met her as a frightened patient with a daughter who spoke for her
You chose a model with her, not for her
You made consent a conversation — and took her tears seriously without calling them incapacity
You kept her information private by default — and asked her, not Dana, how she wanted the truth handled

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Common Mistakes to Avoid

Reducing autonomy to consumerism — handing the patient a menu and calling abandonment “respect for choice.”
Pathologizing disagreement — treating every unwise or emotional decision as proof of lost capacity.
Treating HIPAA as a gag order — shutting out families and stalling care in the name of a rule that does not require it.
Assuming one cultural script — defaulting either to blunt disclosure or to family control instead of asking this patient.
Confusing the form with the process — believing a signature settles a question the conversation never actually reached.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Wrapping Up Part B

Part A explained why modern medicine rejects unchecked paternalism. Part B asked what replaces it: guided, transparent, autonomy-respecting care. Following Mrs. R, we saw the four clinician–patient models, the four elements of valid consent and the case law (Schloendorff → Canterbury) behind them, the difference between an unwise choice and an incapacitated one, and confidentiality and truth-telling as defaults with disciplined, evolving exceptions — from Tarasoff to open notes.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Review Questions
Write a brief answer to each, or use them as discussion prompts.


Recall
What are the four elements of valid informed consent, and why is a signature by itself not enough?


Apply
A patient with kidney failure clearly explains the risks of refusing dialysis, but her son insists that no “reasonable” mother would refuse treatment and asks the team to override her. How should the team assess capacity and respond to the son’s demand?


Debate
Should patients usually get near-immediate electronic access to clinicians’ notes, test interpretations, and differential diagnoses, even when that may increase anxiety, misunderstanding, or conflict?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
References


Appelbaum, Paul S. 2007. “Assessment of Patients’ Competence to Consent to Treatment.” New England Journal of Medicine 357 (18): 1834–40. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMcp074045.


California Court of Appeal. 1986. Bouvia v. Superior Court, 179 Cal. App. 3d 1127.


Cowart, Dax, and Robert Burt. 1998. “Confronting Death: Who Chooses, Who Controls?” Hastings Center Report 28 (1): 14–24. https://doi.org/10.2307/3528337.


Emanuel, Ezekiel J., and Linda L. Emanuel. 1992. “Four Models of the Physician-Patient Relationship.” JAMA 267 (16): 2221–26. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.1992.03480160079038.


Faden, Ruth R., and Tom L. Beauchamp. 1986. A History and Theory of Informed Consent. Oxford University Press.


New York Court of Appeals. 1914. Schloendorff v. Society of New York Hospital, 211 n.y. 125. Opinion of Cardozo, J.


Novack, Dennis H., Robin Plumer, Raymond L. Smith, Herbert Ochitill, Gary R. Morrow, and John M. Bennett. 1979. “Changes in Physicians’ Attitudes Toward Telling the Cancer Patient.” JAMA 241 (9): 897–900. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.1979.03290350017012.


Oken, Donald. 1961. “What to Tell Cancer Patients: A Study of Medical Attitudes.” JAMA 175 (13): 1120–28. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.1961.03040130004002.


Supreme Court of California. 1976. Tarasoff v. Regents of the University of California, 17 Cal. 3d 425 (1976). Supreme Court of California decision.


U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Civil Rights. 2025. Summary of the HIPAA Privacy Rule. HHS. https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/privacy/laws-regulations/index.html.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Part A showed why unchecked professional authority became impossible to defend after Nuremberg, Willowbrook, and Tuskegee.
But the cure cannot simply be “the doctor never decides”: clinicians still hold real expertise, real duties, and real time pressure.
Patients, too, usually come to medicine wanting guidance, not just a list of facts to sort alone.
So the question for Part B is constructive, not just critical: what should replace “doctor knows best”?
The answer this lecture builds is shared, transparent, autonomy-respecting care — and the tools that make it real.





By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

Distinguish the four Emanuel & Emanuel models of the clinician–patient relationship
Explain the four elements of valid informed consent and where consent law came from
Tell an unwise decision apart from an incapacitated one, using a capacity standard
Identify the main limits on confidentiality, including Tarasoff and modern record access
Work through a truth-telling conflict involving family and culture

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Part A showed why unchecked professional authority became impossible to defend after Nuremberg, Willowbrook, and Tuskegee.
But the cure cannot simply be “the doctor never decides”: clinicians still hold real expertise, real duties, and real time pressure.
Patients, too, usually come to medicine wanting guidance, not just a list of facts to sort alone.
So the question for Part B is constructive, not just critical: what should replace “doctor knows best”?
The answer this lecture builds is shared, transparent, autonomy-respecting care — and the tools that make it real.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

Distinguish the four Emanuel & Emanuel models of the clinician–patient relationship
Explain the four elements of valid informed consent and where consent law came from
Tell an unwise decision apart from an incapacitated one, using a capacity standard
Identify the main limits on confidentiality, including Tarasoff and modern record access
Work through a truth-telling conflict involving family and culture

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
2.1 Meet Mrs. R

Mrs. R is 61. She came in for fatigue and weight loss.
The colonoscopy found colon cancer — serious, but treatable.
She is frightened. Her daughter, Dana, drives her to every appointment and does most of the talking.
We will follow Mrs. R through the whole class. Every idea today will land on her.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
2.2 What’s Missing From This Picture?




Sir Luke Fildes, The Doctor (1891), via Wikimedia Commons, public domain.


Fildes’s The Doctor became the most reproduced image of medicine in the English-speaking world, and for a century it was how the profession liked to see itself. The physician watches, interprets, and quietly decides; the parents wait helplessly in the shadows. It is genuinely moving — and that is the trap. The one person with no voice in the picture is the patient, and the entire modern apparatus of consent exists to put that voice back.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
2.3 Three Sources of Asymmetry







asym


P

Patient
ill, vulnerable,
time-pressured



C

Clinician
expertise, options,
interpretation



C->P






I

Institution
policy, cost,
workflow



I->P






F

Family / others
expectations, pressure,
care roles



F->P







 A compact map of the clinical asymmetry: the patient sits at the center, but expertise, institutions, and family pressures all shape the decision space. 



The relationship is hard because the patient is sick, frightened, and often in pain, while the information is unequal from the very first minute. What is at stake is rarely just a technical fact — it is what kind of life is worth living, which is the patient’s question, not the clinician’s. Ethical care does not pretend this asymmetry away; it manages it openly.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
2.4 The Core Tension

Autonomy versus Beneficence

Autonomy is the patient’s right to make informed choices about her own body; beneficence is the clinician’s duty to act for her welfare. Most of the time these run together — good information and a good recommendation are both in the patient’s interest. The hard cases arrive when they pull apart: when what the clinician judges best is exactly what the patient does not want. A competent patient who refuses a recommended surgery has not created a misunderstanding to be fixed; she has created a genuine conflict of values. Almost everything in this lecture is a tool for handling that conflict honestly rather than hiding it.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
3.1 Four Models at a Glance
Ezekiel and Linda Emanuel mapped the clinician–patient relationship into four models in a 1992 JAMA paper that is still the standard reference (Emanuel and Emanuel 1992):








Model
Main clinician role
Main risk




Paternalistic
Decide what is best
Patient becomes passive


Informative
Provide facts and options
Clinician becomes a vending machine


Interpretive
Help clarify the patient’s values
Clinician may over-read the patient


Deliberative
Recommend and reason about values
Persuasion can slide into pressure

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
3.2 One Map of the Models







models


A

How should a clinician
use authority?



B

Patient values
already clear?



A->B





C

If yes, what should
the clinician do?



B->C


yes



P

Paternalistic
clinician chooses
the end



B->P


no



I

Informative
provide facts



C->I


facts only



R

Interpretive
clarify the
patient's values



C->R


clarify values



D

Deliberative
recommend and
give reasons



C->D


recommend values




 The Emanuel models differ mainly in what the clinician does with professional authority once a patient’s values are more or less clear.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
3.3 The Same News, Four Ways (1)
Mrs. R asks: “So what happens now?” Same clinician, same facts, four models:

Paternalistic — “The right move is surgery. We’ll get you scheduled.”

Risk: Mrs. R becomes a passenger in her own care

Informative — “Here are three options with their survival numbers. Which do you want?”

Risk: the facts get dumped; she’s left to interpret them alone

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
3.4 The Same News, Four Ways (2)

Interpretive — “Before we choose: what matters most to you — more time, or fewer side effects?”

Risk: the clinician may over-read what she “really” wants

Deliberative — “Here’s what I’d weigh in your situation, and why. What do you think?”

Risk: good persuasion can tip into pressure



Read these aloud in four different voices. The point students should feel: the facts never changed — only what the clinician did with authority.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
3.5 What the Research Actually Shows

The dominant model today is shared decision-making: the clinician supplies expertise about options and risks, the patient supplies expertise about her own values, and they decide together.
Structured decision aids (booklets, videos, option grids) measurably improve patients’ knowledge and produce choices that better match their values.
But patients differ sharply: most want full information, yet how much decision control they want ranges from “you decide, doctor” to “I decide, period.”
That variation is the empirical case for the slide before — there is no single correct model.
The professional skill is to ask how much steering this patient wants, and to revisit it as illness and confidence change.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
3.6 Which Model Fits?

Match a model to each case — and say why:

An unconscious trauma patient in the ED
A patient choosing between two cancer regimens with similar odds
An older adult unsure whether dialysis still fits her goals
A patient who asks you, “What would you do if it were your mom?”

And: should Mrs. R get the same model at every visit?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
3.7 Make It Contemporary

Mrs. R arrives at the next visit having read everything online. She is convinced she needs a specific drug she saw in an ad, and an AI chatbot agreed with her.

Does the “informative” model still work when a patient can’t judge their own information?
Is telling her the source is wrong paternalistic, or just honest?
What does respecting her autonomy actually require here?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
3.8 After You’ve Discussed






Note




No model wins everywhere. Emergencies push left toward paternalism; chronic illness usually works best in the interpretive/deliberative middle.
Choosing the model is itself a shared decision. The deepest move is to ask the patient how much steering they want — and to revisit it as the illness, and the patient, change. “Advice” with the steering hidden is the move to watch for.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: 4.1 Consent Is a Process, Not a Signature
text:
4.1 Consent Is a Process, Not a Signature

Informed consent is how the abstract principle “respect for persons” becomes a concrete clinical act (Faden and Beauchamp 1986).
A signed form can document that a conversation happened; it can never substitute for the conversation.
A competent refusal is just as valid an exercise of consent as a yes — consent is the right to decide, not the duty to agree.
Outside genuine emergencies, valid consent must come before the intervention, not be backfilled afterward.
The legal doctrine is roughly a century old, and it began not with a form but with a single sentence from a judge.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.2 Where Consent Law Begins


“Every human being of adult years and sound mind has a right to determine what shall be done with his own body; and a surgeon who performs an operation without his patient’s consent commits an assault.”


Justice Benjamin Cardozo, Schloendorff v. Society of New York Hospital (1914) (New York Court of Appeals 1914)


The term “informed consent” itself was not coined until Salgo (1957). The modern standard for how much to disclose was set in Canterbury v. Spence (1972).

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.3 How Much Must You Disclose?








Standard
The test it uses
The problem it has




“Reasonable physician” (older)
Disclose what a typical doctor would disclose
Lets the profession set its own bar


“Reasonable patient” (Canterbury, 1972)
Disclose what a reasonable patient would consider material to the decision
Whose “reasonable patient”?


Subjective / this patient (emerging)
Disclose what this patient would find material
Hard to prove, but closest to real autonomy



Most U.S. jurisdictions now use some version of the Canterbury “reasonable patient” rule — disclosure is measured from the patient’s side of the table, not the doctor’s.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: 4.4 The Four Elements of Valid Consent
text:
4.4 The Four Elements of Valid Consent



Element
The practical question




Disclosure
Was the relevant information provided?


Understanding
Does the patient actually grasp it?


Voluntariness
Is the choice free from controlling pressure?


Capacity
Can the patient make this decision now?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.5 Disclosure & Understanding, with Mrs. R

Disclosure — you explain the cancer, the surgery, the major risks, the alternatives, and what happens if she does nothing.
Understanding — you ask her to say it back: “What did you hear?” She says, “You’re removing part of my colon, and I might need a bag for a while.”
That is real understanding; signing while nodding is not. Studies of recall after consent discussions are sobering — patients routinely forget or misunderstand much of what they were told.
“Teach-back” — having the patient restate the plan in her own words — is the single most reliable, low-tech check that disclosure actually landed.
Use plain language, an interpreter when indicated, and slow down when fear or pain has narrowed her attention.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.6 Voluntariness, with Mrs. R

Dana answers every question before her mother can, and gradually the appointment becomes a conversation between you and the daughter.
Asked directly what she wants, Mrs. R says quietly, “Whatever my daughter thinks is best.”
That sentence is the whole problem in miniature: it may be authentic deference, or it may be pressure wearing the face of family love.
Voluntariness is not destroyed by influence — families always influence — but it is destroyed by control.
A neatly signed form can still document a choice that was never really the patient’s, which is why you sometimes have to see the patient alone.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: 4.7 Capacity Is Not What People Think
text:
4.7 Capacity Is Not What People Think

Capacity is decision-specific and time-specific: a patient can lack it for a complex surgery at 3 a.m. and have it for a simple choice the next morning.
It is not the same as intelligence, a psychiatric diagnosis, or agreeing with the clinician — a person can be floridly eccentric and still have capacity.
It runs on a sliding scale: the graver and more irreversible the consequences, the higher the bar you should require before accepting the decision.
This is why a low-stakes refusal is honored almost automatically while a life-or-death refusal triggers a careful reassessment — appropriately, not as a trick.
Capacity is a clinical judgment about this decision, not a global verdict on the person.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.8 A Practical Capacity Check

The standard clinical test (Appelbaum & Grisso’s four abilities) asks whether the patient can (Appelbaum 2007):

communicate a stable choice
understand the relevant information
appreciate how it applies to them specifically
reason through the options coherently

Failing only the last two — appreciation and reasoning — is what usually distinguishes incapacity from a merely unusual choice.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.9 The Consent Checklist







consent


A

Intervention
proposed



B

Disclosure
adequate?



A->B





C

Understanding
adequate?



B->C


yes



B1

Explain diagnosis,
options, risks,
benefits, alternatives



B->B1


no



D

Voluntary
choice?



C->D


yes



C1

Use teach-back,
plain language,
interpreter, visuals



C->C1


no



E

Capacity for
this decision?



D->E


yes



D1

Address coercion
or pressure



D->D1


no



E1

Seek surrogate or
further assessment



E->E1


no



F

Consent or refusal
is valid



E->F


yes




 A slide-sized informed-consent checklist. Each gate asks a different question, so a signed form alone is never enough.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.10 Refusal Is the Real Test

Easy cases are consent cases: the patient wants what is offered, and everyone relaxes.
The hard cases are refusals, where a competent person says no to something that would clearly help.
An informed refusal demands the same four elements as an informed consent — disclosure, understanding, voluntariness, capacity.
The landmark refusals are concrete: Jehovah’s Witness patients declining life-saving transfusion, and Bouvia v. Superior Court (1986), where a woman with cerebral palsy won the right to refuse force-feeding (California Court of Appeal 1986).
The course’s hardest refusal case, and your paired case study, is Dax Cowart.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.11 Dax Cowart

In 1973 a propane explosion killed Dax Cowart’s father and left Dax, then 25, severely burned and blinded.
Throughout roughly fourteen months of agonizing treatment he repeatedly and lucidly demanded that it stop; clinicians treated him anyway.
He went on to earn a law degree, marry, and become a patient-rights advocate; his case is preserved in the films Please Let Me Die (1974) and Dax’s Case (1985).
Until his death in 2019 he held a deliberately uncomfortable position: he was glad to be alive and insisted his autonomy had been gravely violated.
That paradox — a good outcome reached by a wrong route — is exactly why the case refuses to resolve neatly (Cowart and Burt 1998).


This is the paired case study. Students don’t need the full facts now — only enough to feel the autonomy/beneficence collision before they read it.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.12 Thought Question

A competent adult refuses a blood transfusion that would almost certainly save their life.

Is honoring the refusal respect — or abandonment?
What is the clinician still obligated to keep doing after the refusal?
Mrs. R, in tears, says “I can’t even think about this right now.” Is that a refusal, indecision, or lost capacity — and how would you tell?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.13 Make It Contemporary

Change one fact: the refusal isn’t religious. The patient read online that transfusions are dangerous and won’t budge.

Same legal right. Does the reason change what you owe them?
Does correcting the misinformation respect her autonomy — or undermine it?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.14 After You’ve Discussed






Note




Respecting a decision is not endorsing the reasoning. You can believe she is wrong and still be bound by her refusal.
The capacity bar is applied unevenly. We rarely test capacity when patients say yes; we scrutinize it when they say no. If every risky refusal counted as incapacity, informed consent collapses back into paternalism. The honest question is what danger is enough to re-assess — not to override.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.1 Why Confidentiality Matters

Confidentiality is one of medicine’s oldest promises — it is in the Hippocratic Oath, long before it was in any statute.
Its value is partly instrumental: patients only disclose stigmatized, decision-critical facts (drug use, sexual history, suicidal thoughts) if they trust the information will be held.
Its value is also intrinsic: control over who knows your medical story is part of being treated as a person, not a case.
The evidence is concrete — when adolescents doubt confidentiality, they measurably withhold information and forgo care.
So confidentiality is ethical before it is legal, and breaking it quietly damages care even when no one finds out.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: 5.2 HIPAA Is Not a Gag Order
text:
5.2 HIPAA Is Not a Gag Order

HIPAA is the U.S. federal health-privacy rule, and it is one of the most misunderstood laws in medicine (U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Civil Rights 2025).
It explicitly permits sharing for treatment, payment, and operations, and to avert a serious and imminent threat — it is not a vow of silence.
It runs on a “minimum necessary” principle: access and share only what the task in front of you actually requires.
Misreading it has real costs: families are stonewalled and care coordination suffers because staff wrongly believe HIPAA forbids all disclosure.
Concretely, if Mrs. R’s sister calls for an update, you generally cannot even confirm she is a patient without Mrs. R’s authorization — and that is the rule working as intended.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.3 HHS on the Privacy Rule


“A major goal of the Privacy Rule is to assure that individuals’ health information is properly protected while allowing the flow of health information needed to provide and promote high quality health care and to protect the public’s health and well being. The Rule strikes a balance that permits important uses of information, while protecting the privacy of people who seek care and healing.”


U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Summary of the HIPAA Privacy Rule (U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Civil Rights 2025)

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.4 May Disclose vs. Must Disclose


You may disclose when

The patient authorizes it
It is necessary for treatment
Law permits limited operational use
A serious threat may justify a warning
Legal process requires a response


You must disclose

Suspected child abuse or neglect
Certain infectious diseases
Some court-ordered disclosures
Some state-mandated reporting
(The exact list varies by state)

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.5 Confidentiality: Default and Exceptions







conf


A

Protected health
information



B

Default:
keep private



A->B





C

Patient authorizes
sharing



B->C


exception



D

Law requires
reporting



B->D


exception



E

Serious threat
may justify warning



B->E


exception




 Confidentiality starts from privacy, not disclosure. The legal and ethical question is which exceptions are strong enough to override that default.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: 5.6 The Chart Is Now a Letter to the Patient
text:
5.6 The Chart Is Now a Letter to the Patient

Since 2021, the 21st Century Cures Act’s information-blocking rule gives patients near-immediate electronic access to their notes and most results.
“Open notes” is now the default: the patient will very likely read exactly what you wrote, often before you discuss it.
The upside is real — transparency improves trust, recall, and error-catching, and patients report feeling more in control.
The hard cases are also real: how to document a suspected abuse, a stigmatizing differential, or “patient appears to be a poor historian,” knowing the patient will see it.
The emerging norm is to write the chart as if it were addressed to the patient, because now it effectively is.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.7 Tarasoff: Two Rulings, One Lesson

Prosenjit Poddar, a UC Berkeley student, told campus psychologist Lawrence Moore he intended to kill Tatiana Tarasoff after she rejected him.
Moore alerted campus police, who briefly detained Poddar and released him; no one warned Tatiana or her family, and Poddar killed her in 1969.
The California Supreme Court ruled twice: Tarasoff I (1974) created a duty to warn, and Tarasoff II (1976) broadened it to a duty to protect (Supreme Court of California 1976).
The famous dissent argued the rule would chill therapy and deter the very patients who most need help from speaking freely.
That tradeoff was never settled — it was handed to fifty state legislatures, which is why the duty looks different depending on where you practice.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.8 Tarasoff I vs. Tarasoff II









Tarasoff I (1974)
Tarasoff II (1976)




Duty
Duty to warn the victim
Duty to protect the victim


Options
Tell the identifiable target
Warn, notify police, hospitalize — whatever a reasonable clinician would do


Trigger
A serious threat to an identifiable person
Same — but the response is broader and clinically judged


Today
Some states still use a narrow “warn” rule
Many adopted “protect”; some make it permissive, a few reject it entirely



The exam point: there is no single national Tarasoff rule — only a default ethical question (serious, specific danger to an identifiable person) answered differently by each state.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.9 In the Court’s Words


“We conclude that the public policy favoring protection of the confidential character of patient-psychotherapist communications must yield to the extent to which disclosure is essential to avert danger to others. The protective privilege ends where the public peril begins.”


Tarasoff v. Regents of the University of California (1976) (Supreme Court of California 1976)

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: 5.10 The Case Nobody Warns You About
text:
5.10 The Case Nobody Warns You About

You are a nursing student on a med-surg unit. Your old roommate is admitted two doors down — and you have chart access.
You’re genuinely worried about her. You open her chart “just to see if she’s okay.” You tell no one. No one is harmed.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.11 Snooping Is Not Hypothetical

Every chart access is logged; modern systems run automated audits that flag a record opened by someone not on the care team.
After staff repeatedly accessed celebrities’ records, UCLA’s health system fired employees, faced a federal settlement, and one worker was criminally convicted.
“I only looked, I didn’t tell anyone” is not a defense — the unauthorized access is itself the violation.
For a clinician, the realistic consequences stack: termination, loss of licensure, HIPAA penalties, and in some cases prosecution.
The trigger is almost always personal curiosity about someone the snooper knows or recognizes — exactly the roommate scenario.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.12 Thought Question

Start with the roommate’s chart, then turn the privacy default on its head:

You looked “just to check she’s okay.” What exactly is the wrong, if no one ever finds out?
Does it change if she were your patient? If she’d want you to know? If you tell no one?
Now a patient tells you: “Sometimes I think about killing my ex — I know where she works.” What would you need to know before warning anyone?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.13 Make It Contemporary


Hospitals run automated access audits; every year nurses are fired for snooping on relatives, exes, and celebrities
A patient posts a specific threat on Instagram, not in your office

Does “do I need this to care for this patient?” still draw the line in the digital version — both for the snooping and for the threat?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.14 After You’ve Discussed






Note




Access is not authorization. Being able to open a chart is not permission. The test is “do I need this to care for this patient?” Curiosity, worry, and good intentions are not exceptions.
The default flips, but the question doesn’t. Snooping over-discloses; Tarasoff under-discloses. Both are answered the same way — by asking what is genuinely necessary: to provide care, or to prevent serious, specific harm.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.15 How Truth-Telling Flipped







truth


A

1961 (Oken)
~90% of physicians did NOT
tell a cancer diagnosis



B

1979 (Novack)
~97% of physicians DID
tell the diagnosis



A->B


  one generation  




 In one human generation, the professional norm on disclosing a cancer diagnosis reversed almost completely. 



In 1961 Oken found that roughly nine in ten physicians withheld a cancer diagnosis to “protect” the patient; by 1979 Novack found the norm had almost exactly reversed (Oken 1961; Novack et al. 1979). The lesson is humbling: what felt like obvious kindness was a convention, not a moral fact — and conventions about disclosure still vary widely across cultures.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: 5.16 Back to Mrs. R: “Please Don’t Tell Her”
text:
5.16 Back to Mrs. R: “Please Don’t Tell Her”

The biopsy is worse than expected. Before you go in, Dana stops you in the hallway:

“In our family, we don’t tell elders news like this. It will crush her. Please talk to me, not her.”

The team wants to respect the family’s culture. The team also wants to respect Mrs. R.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.17 Thought Question


Whose decision is this — Mrs. R’s, Dana’s, or the team’s?
What is the first thing you should find out, and who do you ask?
What if you ask Mrs. R and she says, “I don’t want the details — talk to Dana”?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.18 Make It Contemporary

A genetic test shows Mrs. R carries a mutation her adult siblings may also carry.

She does not want them told. They may be making medical decisions without information that could protect them.
Does her privacy outweigh their need to know? Is this the same problem as the family request, or a different one?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.19 After You’ve Discussed






Note




Only Mrs. R can waive her own right to know. A competent patient may autonomously choose not to receive information (“just talk to my daughter”) — that is respected autonomy. The family withholding it from her, unasked, is not their call.
So it isn’t “autonomy vs. culture.” The move is to ask Mrs. R — before the bad news — how much she wants to know and whom she wants involved. Culturally informed, and still hers.
The genetic case is different in kind: a third party’s welfare is now at stake. That is closer to Tarasoff than to truth-telling.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
6.1 Mrs. R, Start to Finish

You met her as a frightened patient with a daughter who spoke for her
You chose a model with her, not for her
You made consent a conversation — and took her tears seriously without calling them incapacity
You kept her information private by default — and asked her, not Dana, how she wanted the truth handled

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
6.2 Common Mistakes to Avoid

Reducing autonomy to consumerism — handing the patient a menu and calling abandonment “respect for choice.”
Pathologizing disagreement — treating every unwise or emotional decision as proof of lost capacity.
Treating HIPAA as a gag order — shutting out families and stalling care in the name of a rule that does not require it.
Assuming one cultural script — defaulting either to blunt disclosure or to family control instead of asking this patient.
Confusing the form with the process — believing a signature settles a question the conversation never actually reached.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
6.3 Wrapping Up Part B

Part A explained why modern medicine rejects unchecked paternalism. Part B asked what replaces it: guided, transparent, autonomy-respecting care. Following Mrs. R, we saw the four clinician–patient models, the four elements of valid consent and the case law (Schloendorff → Canterbury) behind them, the difference between an unwise choice and an incapacitated one, and confidentiality and truth-telling as defaults with disciplined, evolving exceptions — from Tarasoff to open notes.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
6.4 Review Questions
Write a brief answer to each, or use them as discussion prompts.


Recall
What are the four elements of valid informed consent, and why is a signature by itself not enough?


Apply
A patient with kidney failure clearly explains the risks of refusing dialysis, but her son insists that no “reasonable” mother would refuse treatment and asks the team to override her. How should the team assess capacity and respond to the son’s demand?


Debate
Should patients usually get near-immediate electronic access to clinicians’ notes, test interpretations, and differential diagnoses, even when that may increase anxiety, misunderstanding, or conflict?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
6.5 References


Appelbaum, Paul S. 2007. “Assessment of Patients’ Competence to Consent to Treatment.” New England Journal of Medicine 357 (18): 1834–40. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMcp074045.


California Court of Appeal. 1986. Bouvia v. Superior Court, 179 Cal. App. 3d 1127.


Cowart, Dax, and Robert Burt. 1998. “Confronting Death: Who Chooses, Who Controls?” Hastings Center Report 28 (1): 14–24. https://doi.org/10.2307/3528337.


Emanuel, Ezekiel J., and Linda L. Emanuel. 1992. “Four Models of the Physician-Patient Relationship.” JAMA 267 (16): 2221–26. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.1992.03480160079038.


Faden, Ruth R., and Tom L. Beauchamp. 1986. A History and Theory of Informed Consent. Oxford University Press.


New York Court of Appeals. 1914. Schloendorff v. Society of New York Hospital, 211 n.y. 125. Opinion of Cardozo, J.


Novack, Dennis H., Robin Plumer, Raymond L. Smith, Herbert Ochitill, Gary R. Morrow, and John M. Bennett. 1979. “Changes in Physicians’ Attitudes Toward Telling the Cancer Patient.” JAMA 241 (9): 897–900. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.1979.03290350017012.


Oken, Donald. 1961. “What to Tell Cancer Patients: A Study of Medical Attitudes.” JAMA 175 (13): 1120–28. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.1961.03040130004002.


Supreme Court of California. 1976. Tarasoff v. Regents of the University of California, 17 Cal. 3d 425 (1976). Supreme Court of California decision.


U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Civil Rights. 2025. Summary of the HIPAA Privacy Rule. HHS. https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/privacy/laws-regulations/index.html.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The New Question

Part A explained how trials work and how to read their results
The rules that protect participants were written after real abuses
Part B asks a harder question: when does reviewed, legal, well-funded research still wrong people?
One idea organizes the whole lecture: vulnerability — who can be pressured, and who pays the price when knowledge is the goal

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Today

By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

Define vulnerability as a matter of power and pressure, not personality, using a working taxonomy
Apply the Belmont principles as concrete IRB review questions
Explain clinical equipoise as the moral license that both starts and ends a trial
Judge when placebo controls are and are not defensible, including across rich and poor settings
Analyze how commercial funding distorts research, using the Markingson case
Explain why so many published findings fail to replicate — and how AI sharpens the threat

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Three Abuses, Three Protections
Modern research protections were built one scandal at a time.








Case
The wrong
What it forced into place




Nazi experiments
No real consent; extreme harm
Voluntary consent; Nuremberg (Nuremberg Military Tribunals 1947)


Willowbrook
Dependent children, coercive setting
Vulnerable-population protection; independent review (Jonsen 1998)


Tuskegee
Deception, withheld treatment, racist selection
Belmont: respect, beneficence, justice (Brandt 1978; National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1979)



Each row is a failure on the left and the safeguard it forced on the right.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Thought Question

What was the deepest wrong shared by these cases?

People were used as means to an end
People could not refuse freely
Powerful institutions targeted the vulnerable
Researchers treated knowledge as more important than persons


Which answer seems most central to you, and why?
Are these really different wrongs, or different names for one wrong?
Today the abuse rarely looks like camps or segregated wards — it may look like a confusing consent form, or recruiting the people least able to say no. What warning signs would still make you uneasy in a study that is legal, reviewed, and well funded?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Three distinctions are worth keeping sharp:

Consent failures, harm failures, and justice failures are not identical. Tuskegee had all three.
These cases are not only about cruelty. They are also about institutions finding reassuring language for doing the wrong thing.
Modern oversight exists because good intentions are not enough. The point is to distrust self-justification before the damage is done.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Vulnerability Is Pressure, Not Personality

Every case above shares one feature: the people studied could not freely say no
Vulnerability is not a kind of person — it is a kind of situation
The questions that matter: who depends on whom? Who can walk away? What would refusing cost?
Justice cuts both ways: wrongful exploitation of the vulnerable and wrongful exclusion from research are both failures

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
A Working Taxonomy of Vulnerability
Kipnis’s types name what kind of pressure a participant is under — a more useful tool than a list of “vulnerable groups.”








Type
The pressure
Example




Cognitive
Cannot fully understand or weigh the choice
Dementia; acute psychosis


Juridic
Under the authority of someone who wants enrollment
Prisoners; committed patients


Deferential
Socially unable to refuse a trusted figure
Patient recruited by own physician


Medical
Desperate; no good alternative
Terminal illness, “nothing left to try”


Allocational
Poor; the study is a path to care or money
Payment covers rent; trial is the only clinic


Infrastructural
The setting cannot deliver on promises
Weak health system; no post-trial supply

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Why This Frame Matters

The regulated groups — children, prisoners, pregnant people — map onto these types, but the types travel further
A wealthy, educated patient recruited by her own oncologist is deferentially and medically vulnerable, though she is on no protected list
The rest of this lecture is a tour of these pressures: placebo and global trials (allocational, infrastructural), commercial funding (deferential, medical), and bad knowledge (the wronged party becomes the future patient)
Extra protection should not collapse into automatic exclusion — protecting people is not the same as making them invisible

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Thought Question

You are on an IRB reviewing a vaccine study.

Disease burden is high in prisons.
Researchers want to recruit incarcerated people because follow-up will be easy.
They plan a modest payment and better clinic access for participants.


Which types of vulnerability are in play here at once?
Is this fair inclusion, opportunistic targeting, or both?
Would excluding prisoners entirely also create a justice problem?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note



Name the pressures, then design against them. This study mixes juridic (incarceration), allocational (payment, clinic access), and infrastructural (will the vaccine reach them after?) vulnerability.
Convenience is the enemy. “Easy follow-up” is never, by itself, a reason to pick a population.
Inclusion and protection can conflict. Banning prison research protects against coercion but can also deny prisoners the benefits of studies aimed at their own disease burden.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Belmont in One Slide

The Belmont Report (1979) named three principles (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1979)
Respect for persons → real consent; extra care exactly where vulnerability is highest
Beneficence → minimize harm; justify risk against benefit
Justice → who bears the burdens, who gets the benefits
Each becomes a concrete question an IRB must ask of every study

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: One Map from Principle to Protection
text:
One Map from Principle to Protection







belmont


A

Respect for persons



D

Strong consent
and refusal rights



A->D





B

Beneficence



E

Risk-benefit review
and monitoring



B->E





C

Justice



F

Fair subject
selection



C->F






 Belmont principles become protections only when institutions translate them into review questions and protocol requirements.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
You Are the IRB

This protocol lands on your desk:

A 12-month placebo-controlled trial of a new Alzheimer’s drug. Participants: residents of one memory-care facility. Consent: signed by adult children as surrogates. Site chosen because “follow-up is easy — they don’t move.”


Which vulnerability types apply? Score the protocol on each Belmont principle — where is it weakest?
What single change would you require before approving it?
Is “easy follow-up” ever a legitimate reason to pick a population?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note



Respect for persons: these residents are cognitively vulnerable; surrogate consent is allowed, but the residents’ own assent — and dissent — still count. A child’s signature is not the whole story.
Beneficence: a 12-month placebo arm in a progressive disease needs serious justification (US Department of Health and Human Services 2018).
Justice: “they don’t move” is convenience recruitment — exactly what fair selection forbids.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The License to Randomize

Randomizing a sick person is only acceptable under clinical equipoise (Freedman 1987)
Equipoise means the expert community is genuinely uncertain which arm is better
It is not one doctor’s hunch — it is honest, collective “we do not yet know”
If the community already knows, randomizing assigns some patients to the worse arm on purpose
Equipoise is the moral license a trial needs to begin

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Clock Runs Out

Equipoise is not permanent — evidence accumulates while the trial runs
An independent monitoring board can stop a trial early for three reasons:









Trigger
What it means
Why stopping is required




Harm
One arm is clearly hurting people
Continuing knowingly harms participants


Benefit
One arm is clearly better
Keeping the other arm is no longer fair


Futility
The question cannot be answered
Continued risk buys no knowledge




When uncertainty resolves, the moral license expires — the trial must end

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Stopping Early Is Not Free

A counterintuitive fact. Trials stopped early for benefit tend to overestimate how well the treatment works.
Random luck swings hardest in small samples. A trial that stops the moment results look great may have stopped on a lucky high point. This is why monitoring boards use strict statistical rules, not “it looks good enough.” Stopping early is itself an ethical and a scientific judgment.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Placebo Disputes Are Equipoise Disputes

Most fights about placebo controls are really fights about whether equipoise exists
“There is already a proven treatment” = “there is no real uncertainty — so a placebo arm assigns people to a known-worse option”
“The proven treatment cannot actually be delivered here” = “uncertainty about feasible options still exists”
Holding the concept of equipoise lets you see the next debate clearly

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Helsinki’s Placebo Rule

The Declaration of Helsinki says new interventions should usually be tested against the best proven intervention (World Medical Association 2024)
Placebo is acceptable if no proven intervention exists, or for compelling methodological reasons
But not if participants risk serious or irreversible harm by going without proven treatment (World Medical Association 2024)
That last condition is the one people fight about — and it is an equipoise fight

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: The AZT Trials in Five Lines
text:
The AZT Trials in Five Lines

In 1994, a long-course zidovudine regimen reduced perinatal HIV transmission
That regimen was expensive and logistically hard in many low-resource settings
Later trials tested shorter, cheaper regimens, often against placebo
Critics: this withheld a known effective intervention (Lurie and Wolfe 1997)
Defenders: the real question was what feasible strategy could work where the long regimen was unavailable (Varmus and Satcher 1997)

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Two Famous Positions


Lurie and Wolfe (Lurie and Wolfe 1997)

A proven regimen already existed
Placebo meant withholding known benefit
Poverty must not lower the ethical standard
Poorer countries should not get weaker protections


Varmus and Satcher (Varmus and Satcher 1997)

The long regimen was not realistically implementable
A shorter regimen might still save lives at scale
The relevant comparison had to include feasibility
Blocking the trial preserves principle but blocks benefit

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
A Placebo Decision Tree







placebo


A

Does a best proven
intervention exist?



B

Placebo may be
ethically easier to justify



A->B


no



C

Would withholding it risk
serious or irreversible harm?



A->C


yes



D

Placebo is a major
ethical red flag



C->D


yes



E

Is placebo genuinely
methodologically necessary?



C->E


no



F

Use active-control
design instead



E->F


no



G

Approve only with
strong justification and protections



E->G


yes




 A simplified placebo decision tree. Each diamond is, in effect, a question about whether genuine equipoise exists. Real IRB review is more detailed.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Thought Question

You are on an ethics committee in 1997 reviewing a short-course AZT trial in Uganda.

In the sponsor country, the long-course regimen already works.
In the host country, that regimen is too expensive and complex for ordinary use.
The proposed study compares a shorter regimen to placebo.


Is there genuine equipoise here — and equipoise about what?
Does local unavailability change what researchers ethically owe participants?
What is owed after the study — do participants keep what worked?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Three hard truths belong together here — this is allocational and infrastructural vulnerability by design:

“Local standard” describes the world; it does not settle what is ethically owed.
Feasibility matters, but poverty must not become a discount on human protections. A study can be practical and still exploitative.
Post-trial access is part of the ethics, not cleanup. Proving a regimen works and then withdrawing it uses the host community (Emanuel et al. 2004; World Medical Association 2024).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Henrietta Lacks

In 1951, Henrietta Lacks, a Black woman, was treated for cervical cancer
Without her knowledge, cells from her tumor were taken and grown
They became HeLa — the first immortal human cell line, used in countless studies and products
She was never asked; her family was not told for decades and shared in none of the benefit
No drug, no placebo, no “arm” — and still a major research-ethics failure: a vulnerability of never being asked at all

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
What the Rules Now Require

US human-subjects research follows the Common Rule, rewritten in 2018 (US Department of Health and Human Services 2018)
Consent forms must open with a short “key information” summary in plain language
Broad consent: a person can agree once to future, unspecified research on stored samples and data
Most multi-site studies now use a single IRB instead of one per site
Broad consent is more honest than silence — but ethically thinner than asking each time

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Thought Question

You give a blood sample for a routine test. A leftover vial is stored. Years later it is used — deidentified — in genetic research and a profitable diagnostic. You were never specifically asked; a broad intake form said “samples may be used for research.”

Was that consent meaningful?
Do you have a claim to a say — or a share — in what was discovered?
Is “deidentified” enough, when the sample is DNA that can re-identify you and implicate relatives?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note



Consent for this study is not consent for all future research. Tissue and data banks rely on broad consent — useful, but ethically thinner than specific consent.
Benefit-sharing is part of justice. If people make a discovery possible, who profits is an ethical question, not an afterthought.
“Deidentified” weakens but does not erase the stake — genomic data can re-identify, and can implicate relatives who never agreed.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Structural Conflict

Most clinical trials are now funded by the companies that profit if the drug succeeds
Industry funding is not wrong by itself — but it builds in a conflict of interest
Watch three roles: who funds, who recruits, and who reviews
Danger peaks when the same people, paid by the sponsor, also treat and enroll the patient
This is deferential and medical vulnerability meeting a financial incentive

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Markingson Case

Dan Markingson, University of Minnesota, 2003–2004. A young man with acute psychosis was placed under an involuntary commitment order. He was enrolled by his treating psychiatrist into the industry-funded CAFÉ antipsychotic trial — while facing commitment to a state hospital if he did not cooperate with his treatment team. His mother repeatedly warned that he was deteriorating. He died by suicide during the study.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
What the 2015 Review Found

Markingson was, in the state auditor’s words, “extraordinarily vulnerable” — committed, psychotic, with no real freedom to refuse (Office of the Legislative Auditor, State of Minnesota 2015)
Investigators and the IRB chair had financial ties to the sponsor, creating an incentive to enroll and retain subjects (Office of the Legislative Auditor, State of Minnesota 2015)
The University’s review of his death was superficial — it did not examine medical records or seek outside information
Bioethicist Carl Elliott spent years pressing for accountability against institutional resistance (Elliott 2010)
Every vulnerability type from earlier is here at once: cognitive, juridic, deferential, medical

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Disclosure Is Not a Firewall
A well-designed system does not rely on good intentions. It builds structure:







Weak safeguard
Stronger safeguard




Investigator discloses COI on a form
Recruitment done by someone with no stake in enrollment


Sponsor analyzes its own data
Independent monitoring board and statisticians


Treating doctor also enrolls the patient
Separation of the care role from the research role


Institution investigates itself
External, empowered review when harm occurs

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Thought Question

A patient newly diagnosed with a serious illness is offered a trial by the physician she trusts most — her own specialist. The specialist receives per-patient payments from the sponsor for each enrollee.

Which vulnerability types is she under, before any form is signed?
Is disclosure of the payment enough to fix the problem? Why or why not?
What single structural change would most reduce the conflict?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note



Conflict of interest is structural, not just moral. Honest people in a bad structure still produce biased enrollment.
Disclosure is the floor, not the fix. Telling someone about a conflict does not neutralize it; separating the roles does.
The treating clinician is the highest-pressure recruiter. That is exactly why independent recruitment and review exist.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Protecting Participants Is Not Enough

A clean consent process does not guarantee trustworthy knowledge
The US Office of Research Integrity names three core wrongs:

Fabrication — making up data
Falsification — manipulating data, images, or results
Plagiarism — passing off others’ work as your own

A faked study can change treatment for millions before anyone notices — the wronged party is now the future patient

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: Why So Many Findings Are False
text:
Why So Many Findings Are False
A landmark argument: under realistic conditions, most published findings can be wrong (Ioannidis 2005). Four ingredients:

Low base rate — most hypotheses tested are false to begin with
Small studies — easily swung by chance (recall: small samples are fragile)
Researcher flexibility — many small choices, each nudging toward “significant”
Publication bias — positive results get published; null results vanish

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: How Flexibility Becomes a False Result
text:
How Flexibility Becomes a False Result

A worked example. Test one hypothesis at p < 0.05 and a false positive is unlikely. Now test 20 subgroups and report only the one that “worked”: a positive result is now likely by pure chance.

p-hacking — trying analyses until one crosses the line
HARKing — Hypothesizing After the Results are Known, then writing it up as if predicted

Neither requires lying. Both manufacture false findings (Simmons et al. 2011).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
It Is Systemic, Not Rare

Psychology: a large replication project reproduced only about 39% of published findings (Open Science Collaboration 2015)
Preclinical cancer: one team could confirm just 6 of 53 landmark studies (Begley and Ellis 2012)
These are not fringe journals — they are the literature clinicians rely on
“Published” and “peer-reviewed” are a floor, not a guarantee

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Alzheimer’s Case, in Full

In 2006, a Nature paper named a specific amyloid protein, **Aβ*56, as a cause of memory loss — it became one of the most-cited Alzheimer’s papers of its era and helped lock in the “amyloid hypothesis” that steered roughly 16 years** of funding and drug development.
In 2022, a six-month Science investigation found that key images in that paper, and in many related ones, appear to have been manipulated (Piller 2022).


Through those years, amyloid-targeting drugs failed trial after trial
Participants in those trials received infusions with real risks — brain swelling and microbleeds
A possibly fabricated result may have redirected a field while patients volunteered for drugs built on it

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: Psychology: Famous Results That Didn’t Hold
text:
Psychology: Famous Results That Didn’t Hold
The replication project that reproduced only ~39% of findings (Open Science Collaboration 2015) swept up results students may already know:

Power posing — holding a “confident” stance was said to change hormones and risk-taking; large replications found no reliable effect
Ego depletion — willpower as a fuel that “runs out”; a 23-lab preregistered test found the effect near zero
Social priming — reading “elderly” words said to make people walk slower; did not replicate

These were not fringe claims. They were TED talks, textbooks, and bestsellers.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: Medicine: Treatments Patients Received That Didn’t Work
text:
Medicine: Treatments Patients Received That Didn’t Work
One review found about 40% of established practices tested over a decade were later contradicted (Prasad et al. 2013).








Treatment
What patients went through
What rigorous trials showed




Knee arthroscopy for arthritis
Real surgery, anesthesia, recovery
No better than sham surgery (Moseley et al. 2002)


Hormone therapy “for the heart”
Years of daily medication
Raised heart disease, stroke, breast cancer (Writing Group for the Women’s Health Initiative Investigators 2002)


Vertebroplasty (spinal fracture)
Cement injected into the spine
No better than a sham procedure


Stents for stable chest pain
An invasive cardiac procedure
No better than medication for symptoms



Each was standard, reimbursed, and sincerely recommended. Bad evidence is not abstract — it is surgery and side effects that did nothing.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The AI Turn

Surgisphere, 2020: The Lancet and NEJM retracted COVID papers built on a huge hospital database that no one could audit and that likely never existed — fabricated big data, accepted because it looked authoritative
AI now lowers the cost of exactly this: fabricated images, synthetic “data,” and fluent paper-mill manuscripts at scale
It also makes p-hacking faster — automated search across thousands of model variants
Counter-tools exist (image forensics, statistical screening), but detection lags creation
For a clinician: provenance and replication matter more than polish; a confident, well-written result is not a true one

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Thought Question

A landmark drug trial is later found to contain manipulated data. Thousands of patients were treated based on it. (Recall the opioid story from Part A: a single misread letter, heavily promoted, helped launch a crisis.)

Who was wronged — the study’s participants, future patients, or both?
Is faking data a worse research-ethics violation than a consent failure, or just a different one?
Why does the system usually catch this slowly — and what would catch it sooner?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note



Integrity is a subject-protection issue too. A false result is not just an academic error — it is the knee surgery, the hormone pill, the amyloid infusion that thousands of real people endured for nothing.
The safeguards are thin by design. Science runs on trust and infrequent replication; misconduct — and now AI — exploits exactly that.
Fixes are structural: preregistration, registered reports, data sharing, independent replication, faster retractions.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Through-Line

One question ran through everything: who could be pressured, and who pays when knowledge is the goal?
Vulnerability is a kind of situation, not a kind of person
Consent, equipoise, fair selection, conflict-of-interest firewalls, and research integrity are all answers to that one question
The rulebook exists to keep learning answerable to persons — during the study, after it, and in what the study is allowed to claim

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Wrapping Up Part B

Research ethics is the systematic study of how the pursuit of knowledge can wrong people — and how to design against it. Vulnerability, understood as power and pressure rather than personality, is the spine: it explains why consent must be real, why equipoise both licenses and ends a trial, why placebo and global trials turn on feasibility and post-trial duties, why commercial funding needs structural firewalls rather than mere disclosure, and why a replication crisis sharpened by AI is itself a subject-protection problem. Ethical research is not research with a signature. It is research designed so that the people who make knowledge possible — and the future patients who will rely on it — are treated as persons first.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Review Questions
Write a brief answer to each, or use them as discussion prompts.


Recall
How does the Belmont principle of Justice translate into the concrete IRB requirement of Fair Subject Selection, and what is the difference between cognitive and allocational vulnerability in Kipnis’s taxonomy?


Apply
A biotech company wants to compile a large registry of genomic data from children in foster care to study rare pediatric neurological conditions. Consent will be obtained from state caseworkers, and the children’s data will be fully de-identified. Based on Kipnis’s types of vulnerability and the Belmont principles, identify two distinct ethical concerns that an IRB should address before approving this protocol.


Debate
In global clinical research, does the local unavailability of a proven treatment justify using a placebo-controlled design in a low-resource host country, or does this practice represent a double standard that exploits structural global inequalities?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
References

Emanuel, Ezekiel J., David Wendler, Jack Killen, and Christine Grady. 2004. “What Makes Clinical Research in Developing Countries Ethical? The Benchmarks of Ethical Research.” The Journal of Infectious Diseases 189 (5): 930–37. https://doi.org/10.1086/381709.

The Belmont Report: Ethical Principles and Guidelines for the Protection of Human Subjects of Research. Open Science Collaboration. 2015. “Estimating the Reproducibility of Psychological Science.” Science 349 (6251): aac4716. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.aac4716.


Piller, Charles. 2022. “Blots on a Field?” Science 377 (6604): 358–63. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.add9993.


Prasad, Vinay, Andrae Vandross, Caitlin Toomey, et al. 2013. “A Decade of Reversal: An Analysis of 146 Contradicted Medical Practices.” Mayo Clinic Proceedings 88 (8): 790–98. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mayocp.2013.05.012.


Simmons, Joseph P., Leif D. Nelson, and Uri Simonsohn. 2011. “False-Positive Psychology: Undisclosed Flexibility in Data Collection and Analysis Allows Presenting Anything as Significant.” Psychological Science 22 (11): 1359–66. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797611417632.


US Department of Health and Human Services. 2018. Federal Policy for the Protection of Human Subjects (“the Common Rule”). Code of Federal Regulations, Title 45, Part 46. https://www.hhs.gov/ohrp/regulations-and-policy/regulations/common-rule/index.html.
