title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Part A explained how trials work and how to read their results
The rules that protect participants were written after real abuses
Part B asks a harder question: when does reviewed, legal, well-funded research still wrong people?
One idea organizes the whole lecture: vulnerability — who can be pressured, and who pays the price when knowledge is the goal





By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

Define vulnerability as a matter of power and pressure, not personality, using a working taxonomy
Apply the Belmont principles as concrete IRB review questions
Explain clinical equipoise as the moral license that both starts and ends a trial
Judge when placebo controls are and are not defensible, including across rich and poor settings
Analyze how commercial funding distorts research, using the Markingson case
Explain why so many published findings fail to replicate — and how AI sharpens the threat

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Part A explained how trials work and how to read their results
The rules that protect participants were written after real abuses
Part B asks a harder question: when does reviewed, legal, well-funded research still wrong people?
One idea organizes the whole lecture: vulnerability — who can be pressured, and who pays the price when knowledge is the goal

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

Define vulnerability as a matter of power and pressure, not personality, using a working taxonomy
Apply the Belmont principles as concrete IRB review questions
Explain clinical equipoise as the moral license that both starts and ends a trial
Judge when placebo controls are and are not defensible, including across rich and poor settings
Analyze how commercial funding distorts research, using the Markingson case
Explain why so many published findings fail to replicate — and how AI sharpens the threat

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
2.1 Three Abuses, Three Protections
Modern research protections were built one scandal at a time.








Case
The wrong
What it forced into place




Nazi experiments
No real consent; extreme harm
Voluntary consent; Nuremberg (Nuremberg Military Tribunals 1947)


Willowbrook
Dependent children, coercive setting
Vulnerable-population protection; independent review (Jonsen 1998)


Tuskegee
Deception, withheld treatment, racist selection
Belmont: respect, beneficence, justice (Brandt 1978; National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1979)



Each row is a failure on the left and the safeguard it forced on the right.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
2.2 Thought Question

What was the deepest wrong shared by these cases?

People were used as means to an end
People could not refuse freely
Powerful institutions targeted the vulnerable
Researchers treated knowledge as more important than persons


Which answer seems most central to you, and why?
Are these really different wrongs, or different names for one wrong?
Today the abuse rarely looks like camps or segregated wards — it may look like a confusing consent form, or recruiting the people least able to say no. What warning signs would still make you uneasy in a study that is legal, reviewed, and well funded?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
2.3 After You’ve Discussed






Note



Three distinctions are worth keeping sharp:

Consent failures, harm failures, and justice failures are not identical. Tuskegee had all three.
These cases are not only about cruelty. They are also about institutions finding reassuring language for doing the wrong thing.
Modern oversight exists because good intentions are not enough. The point is to distrust self-justification before the damage is done.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.1 Vulnerability Is Pressure, Not Personality

Every case above shares one feature: the people studied could not freely say no
Vulnerability is not a kind of person — it is a kind of situation
The questions that matter: who depends on whom? Who can walk away? What would refusing cost?
Justice cuts both ways: wrongful exploitation of the vulnerable and wrongful exclusion from research are both failures

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.2 A Working Taxonomy of Vulnerability
Kipnis’s types name what kind of pressure a participant is under — a more useful tool than a list of “vulnerable groups.”








Type
The pressure
Example




Cognitive
Cannot fully understand or weigh the choice
Dementia; acute psychosis


Juridic
Under the authority of someone who wants enrollment
Prisoners; committed patients


Deferential
Socially unable to refuse a trusted figure
Patient recruited by own physician


Medical
Desperate; no good alternative
Terminal illness, “nothing left to try”


Allocational
Poor; the study is a path to care or money
Payment covers rent; trial is the only clinic


Infrastructural
The setting cannot deliver on promises
Weak health system; no post-trial supply

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.3 Why This Frame Matters

The regulated groups — children, prisoners, pregnant people — map onto these types, but the types travel further
A wealthy, educated patient recruited by her own oncologist is deferentially and medically vulnerable, though she is on no protected list
The rest of this lecture is a tour of these pressures: placebo and global trials (allocational, infrastructural), commercial funding (deferential, medical), and bad knowledge (the wronged party becomes the future patient)
Extra protection should not collapse into automatic exclusion — protecting people is not the same as making them invisible

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.4 Thought Question

You are on an IRB reviewing a vaccine study.

Disease burden is high in prisons.
Researchers want to recruit incarcerated people because follow-up will be easy.
They plan a modest payment and better clinic access for participants.


Which types of vulnerability are in play here at once?
Is this fair inclusion, opportunistic targeting, or both?
Would excluding prisoners entirely also create a justice problem?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.5 After You’ve Discussed






Note




Name the pressures, then design against them. This study mixes juridic (incarceration), allocational (payment, clinic access), and infrastructural (will the vaccine reach them after?) vulnerability.
Convenience is the enemy. “Easy follow-up” is never, by itself, a reason to pick a population.
Inclusion and protection can conflict. Banning prison research protects against coercion but can also deny prisoners the benefits of studies aimed at their own disease burden.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.1 Belmont in One Slide

The Belmont Report (1979) named three principles (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1979)
Respect for persons → real consent; extra care exactly where vulnerability is highest
Beneficence → minimize harm; justify risk against benefit
Justice → who bears the burdens, who gets the benefits
Each becomes a concrete question an IRB must ask of every study

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 4.2 One Map from Principle to Protection
text:
4.2 One Map from Principle to Protection







belmont


A

Respect for persons



D

Strong consent
and refusal rights



A->D





B

Beneficence



E

Risk-benefit review
and monitoring



B->E





C

Justice



F

Fair subject
selection



C->F






 Belmont principles become protections only when institutions translate them into review questions and protocol requirements.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.3 You Are the IRB

This protocol lands on your desk:

A 12-month placebo-controlled trial of a new Alzheimer’s drug. Participants: residents of one memory-care facility. Consent: signed by adult children as surrogates. Site chosen because “follow-up is easy — they don’t move.”


Which vulnerability types apply? Score the protocol on each Belmont principle — where is it weakest?
What single change would you require before approving it?
Is “easy follow-up” ever a legitimate reason to pick a population?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.4 After You’ve Discussed






Note




Respect for persons: these residents are cognitively vulnerable; surrogate consent is allowed, but the residents’ own assent — and dissent — still count. A child’s signature is not the whole story.
Beneficence: a 12-month placebo arm in a progressive disease needs serious justification (US Department of Health and Human Services 2018).
Justice: “they don’t move” is convenience recruitment — exactly what fair selection forbids.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
5.1 The License to Randomize

Randomizing a sick person is only acceptable under clinical equipoise (Freedman 1987)
Equipoise means the expert community is genuinely uncertain which arm is better
It is not one doctor’s hunch — it is honest, collective “we do not yet know”
If the community already knows, randomizing assigns some patients to the worse arm on purpose
Equipoise is the moral license a trial needs to begin

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
5.2 The Clock Runs Out

Equipoise is not permanent — evidence accumulates while the trial runs
An independent monitoring board can stop a trial early for three reasons:









Trigger
What it means
Why stopping is required




Harm
One arm is clearly hurting people
Continuing knowingly harms participants


Benefit
One arm is clearly better
Keeping the other arm is no longer fair


Futility
The question cannot be answered
Continued risk buys no knowledge




When uncertainty resolves, the moral license expires — the trial must end

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
5.3 Stopping Early Is Not Free

A counterintuitive fact. Trials stopped early for benefit tend to overestimate how well the treatment works.
Random luck swings hardest in small samples. A trial that stops the moment results look great may have stopped on a lucky high point. This is why monitoring boards use strict statistical rules, not “it looks good enough.” Stopping early is itself an ethical and a scientific judgment.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
5.4 Placebo Disputes Are Equipoise Disputes

Most fights about placebo controls are really fights about whether equipoise exists
“There is already a proven treatment” = “there is no real uncertainty — so a placebo arm assigns people to a known-worse option”
“The proven treatment cannot actually be delivered here” = “uncertainty about feasible options still exists”
Holding the concept of equipoise lets you see the next debate clearly

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.1 Helsinki’s Placebo Rule

The Declaration of Helsinki says new interventions should usually be tested against the best proven intervention (World Medical Association 2024)
Placebo is acceptable if no proven intervention exists, or for compelling methodological reasons
But not if participants risk serious or irreversible harm by going without proven treatment (World Medical Association 2024)
That last condition is the one people fight about — and it is an equipoise fight

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 6.2 The AZT Trials in Five Lines
text:
6.2 The AZT Trials in Five Lines

In 1994, a long-course zidovudine regimen reduced perinatal HIV transmission
That regimen was expensive and logistically hard in many low-resource settings
Later trials tested shorter, cheaper regimens, often against placebo
Critics: this withheld a known effective intervention (Lurie and Wolfe 1997)
Defenders: the real question was what feasible strategy could work where the long regimen was unavailable (Varmus and Satcher 1997)

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.3 Two Famous Positions


Lurie and Wolfe (Lurie and Wolfe 1997)

A proven regimen already existed
Placebo meant withholding known benefit
Poverty must not lower the ethical standard
Poorer countries should not get weaker protections


Varmus and Satcher (Varmus and Satcher 1997)

The long regimen was not realistically implementable
A shorter regimen might still save lives at scale
The relevant comparison had to include feasibility
Blocking the trial preserves principle but blocks benefit

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.4 A Placebo Decision Tree







placebo


A

Does a best proven
intervention exist?



B

Placebo may be
ethically easier to justify



A->B


no



C

Would withholding it risk
serious or irreversible harm?



A->C


yes



D

Placebo is a major
ethical red flag



C->D


yes



E

Is placebo genuinely
methodologically necessary?



C->E


no



F

Use active-control
design instead



E->F


no



G

Approve only with
strong justification and protections



E->G


yes




 A simplified placebo decision tree. Each diamond is, in effect, a question about whether genuine equipoise exists. Real IRB review is more detailed.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.5 Thought Question

You are on an ethics committee in 1997 reviewing a short-course AZT trial in Uganda.

In the sponsor country, the long-course regimen already works.
In the host country, that regimen is too expensive and complex for ordinary use.
The proposed study compares a shorter regimen to placebo.


Is there genuine equipoise here — and equipoise about what?
Does local unavailability change what researchers ethically owe participants?
What is owed after the study — do participants keep what worked?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.6 After You’ve Discussed






Note



Three hard truths belong together here — this is allocational and infrastructural vulnerability by design:

“Local standard” describes the world; it does not settle what is ethically owed.
Feasibility matters, but poverty must not become a discount on human protections. A study can be practical and still exploitative.
Post-trial access is part of the ethics, not cleanup. Proving a regimen works and then withdrawing it uses the host community (Emanuel et al. 2004; World Medical Association 2024).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
7.1 Henrietta Lacks

In 1951, Henrietta Lacks, a Black woman, was treated for cervical cancer
Without her knowledge, cells from her tumor were taken and grown
They became HeLa — the first immortal human cell line, used in countless studies and products
She was never asked; her family was not told for decades and shared in none of the benefit
No drug, no placebo, no “arm” — and still a major research-ethics failure: a vulnerability of never being asked at all

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
7.2 What the Rules Now Require

US human-subjects research follows the Common Rule, rewritten in 2018 (US Department of Health and Human Services 2018)
Consent forms must open with a short “key information” summary in plain language
Broad consent: a person can agree once to future, unspecified research on stored samples and data
Most multi-site studies now use a single IRB instead of one per site
Broad consent is more honest than silence — but ethically thinner than asking each time

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
7.3 Thought Question

You give a blood sample for a routine test. A leftover vial is stored. Years later it is used — deidentified — in genetic research and a profitable diagnostic. You were never specifically asked; a broad intake form said “samples may be used for research.”

Was that consent meaningful?
Do you have a claim to a say — or a share — in what was discovered?
Is “deidentified” enough, when the sample is DNA that can re-identify you and implicate relatives?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
7.4 After You’ve Discussed






Note




Consent for this study is not consent for all future research. Tissue and data banks rely on broad consent — useful, but ethically thinner than specific consent.
Benefit-sharing is part of justice. If people make a discovery possible, who profits is an ethical question, not an afterthought.
“Deidentified” weakens but does not erase the stake — genomic data can re-identify, and can implicate relatives who never agreed.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
8.1 The Structural Conflict

Most clinical trials are now funded by the companies that profit if the drug succeeds
Industry funding is not wrong by itself — but it builds in a conflict of interest
Watch three roles: who funds, who recruits, and who reviews
Danger peaks when the same people, paid by the sponsor, also treat and enroll the patient
This is deferential and medical vulnerability meeting a financial incentive

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
8.2 The Markingson Case

Dan Markingson, University of Minnesota, 2003–2004. A young man with acute psychosis was placed under an involuntary commitment order. He was enrolled by his treating psychiatrist into the industry-funded CAFÉ antipsychotic trial — while facing commitment to a state hospital if he did not cooperate with his treatment team. His mother repeatedly warned that he was deteriorating. He died by suicide during the study.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
8.3 What the 2015 Review Found

Markingson was, in the state auditor’s words, “extraordinarily vulnerable” — committed, psychotic, with no real freedom to refuse (Office of the Legislative Auditor, State of Minnesota 2015)
Investigators and the IRB chair had financial ties to the sponsor, creating an incentive to enroll and retain subjects (Office of the Legislative Auditor, State of Minnesota 2015)
The University’s review of his death was superficial — it did not examine medical records or seek outside information
Bioethicist Carl Elliott spent years pressing for accountability against institutional resistance (Elliott 2010)
Every vulnerability type from earlier is here at once: cognitive, juridic, deferential, medical

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
8.4 Disclosure Is Not a Firewall
A well-designed system does not rely on good intentions. It builds structure:







Weak safeguard
Stronger safeguard




Investigator discloses COI on a form
Recruitment done by someone with no stake in enrollment


Sponsor analyzes its own data
Independent monitoring board and statisticians


Treating doctor also enrolls the patient
Separation of the care role from the research role


Institution investigates itself
External, empowered review when harm occurs

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
8.5 Thought Question

A patient newly diagnosed with a serious illness is offered a trial by the physician she trusts most — her own specialist. The specialist receives per-patient payments from the sponsor for each enrollee.

Which vulnerability types is she under, before any form is signed?
Is disclosure of the payment enough to fix the problem? Why or why not?
What single structural change would most reduce the conflict?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
8.6 After You’ve Discussed






Note




Conflict of interest is structural, not just moral. Honest people in a bad structure still produce biased enrollment.
Disclosure is the floor, not the fix. Telling someone about a conflict does not neutralize it; separating the roles does.
The treating clinician is the highest-pressure recruiter. That is exactly why independent recruitment and review exist.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
9.1 Protecting Participants Is Not Enough

A clean consent process does not guarantee trustworthy knowledge
The US Office of Research Integrity names three core wrongs:

Fabrication — making up data
Falsification — manipulating data, images, or results
Plagiarism — passing off others’ work as your own

A faked study can change treatment for millions before anyone notices — the wronged party is now the future patient

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 9.2 Why So Many Findings Are False
text:
9.2 Why So Many Findings Are False
A landmark argument: under realistic conditions, most published findings can be wrong (Ioannidis 2005). Four ingredients:

Low base rate — most hypotheses tested are false to begin with
Small studies — easily swung by chance (recall: small samples are fragile)
Researcher flexibility — many small choices, each nudging toward “significant”
Publication bias — positive results get published; null results vanish

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 9.3 How Flexibility Becomes a False Result
text:
9.3 How Flexibility Becomes a False Result

A worked example. Test one hypothesis at p < 0.05 and a false positive is unlikely. Now test 20 subgroups and report only the one that “worked”: a positive result is now likely by pure chance.

p-hacking — trying analyses until one crosses the line
HARKing — Hypothesizing After the Results are Known, then writing it up as if predicted

Neither requires lying. Both manufacture false findings (Simmons et al. 2011).

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
9.4 It Is Systemic, Not Rare

Psychology: a large replication project reproduced only about 39% of published findings (Open Science Collaboration 2015)
Preclinical cancer: one team could confirm just 6 of 53 landmark studies (Begley and Ellis 2012)
These are not fringe journals — they are the literature clinicians rely on
“Published” and “peer-reviewed” are a floor, not a guarantee

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
9.5 The Alzheimer’s Case, in Full

In 2006, a Nature paper named a specific amyloid protein, **Aβ*56, as a cause of memory loss — it became one of the most-cited Alzheimer’s papers of its era and helped lock in the “amyloid hypothesis” that steered roughly 16 years** of funding and drug development.
In 2022, a six-month Science investigation found that key images in that paper, and in many related ones, appear to have been manipulated (Piller 2022).


Through those years, amyloid-targeting drugs failed trial after trial
Participants in those trials received infusions with real risks — brain swelling and microbleeds
A possibly fabricated result may have redirected a field while patients volunteered for drugs built on it

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 9.6 Psychology: Famous Results That Didn’t Hold
text:
9.6 Psychology: Famous Results That Didn’t Hold
The replication project that reproduced only ~39% of findings (Open Science Collaboration 2015) swept up results students may already know:

Power posing — holding a “confident” stance was said to change hormones and risk-taking; large replications found no reliable effect
Ego depletion — willpower as a fuel that “runs out”; a 23-lab preregistered test found the effect near zero
Social priming — reading “elderly” words said to make people walk slower; did not replicate

These were not fringe claims. They were TED talks, textbooks, and bestsellers.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 9.7 Medicine: Treatments Patients Received That Didn’t Work
text:
9.7 Medicine: Treatments Patients Received That Didn’t Work
One review found about 40% of established practices tested over a decade were later contradicted (Prasad et al. 2013).








Treatment
What patients went through
What rigorous trials showed




Knee arthroscopy for arthritis
Real surgery, anesthesia, recovery
No better than sham surgery (Moseley et al. 2002)


Hormone therapy “for the heart”
Years of daily medication
Raised heart disease, stroke, breast cancer (Writing Group for the Women’s Health Initiative Investigators 2002)


Vertebroplasty (spinal fracture)
Cement injected into the spine
No better than a sham procedure


Stents for stable chest pain
An invasive cardiac procedure
No better than medication for symptoms



Each was standard, reimbursed, and sincerely recommended. Bad evidence is not abstract — it is surgery and side effects that did nothing.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
9.8 The AI Turn

Surgisphere, 2020: The Lancet and NEJM retracted COVID papers built on a huge hospital database that no one could audit and that likely never existed — fabricated big data, accepted because it looked authoritative
AI now lowers the cost of exactly this: fabricated images, synthetic “data,” and fluent paper-mill manuscripts at scale
It also makes p-hacking faster — automated search across thousands of model variants
Counter-tools exist (image forensics, statistical screening), but detection lags creation
For a clinician: provenance and replication matter more than polish; a confident, well-written result is not a true one

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
9.9 Thought Question

A landmark drug trial is later found to contain manipulated data. Thousands of patients were treated based on it. (Recall the opioid story from Part A: a single misread letter, heavily promoted, helped launch a crisis.)

Who was wronged — the study’s participants, future patients, or both?
Is faking data a worse research-ethics violation than a consent failure, or just a different one?
Why does the system usually catch this slowly — and what would catch it sooner?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
9.10 After You’ve Discussed






Note




Integrity is a subject-protection issue too. A false result is not just an academic error — it is the knee surgery, the hormone pill, the amyloid infusion that thousands of real people endured for nothing.
The safeguards are thin by design. Science runs on trust and infrequent replication; misconduct — and now AI — exploits exactly that.
Fixes are structural: preregistration, registered reports, data sharing, independent replication, faster retractions.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
10.1 The Through-Line

One question ran through everything: who could be pressured, and who pays when knowledge is the goal?
Vulnerability is a kind of situation, not a kind of person
Consent, equipoise, fair selection, conflict-of-interest firewalls, and research integrity are all answers to that one question
The rulebook exists to keep learning answerable to persons — during the study, after it, and in what the study is allowed to claim

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
10.2 Wrapping Up Part B

Research ethics is the systematic study of how the pursuit of knowledge can wrong people — and how to design against it. Vulnerability, understood as power and pressure rather than personality, is the spine: it explains why consent must be real, why equipoise both licenses and ends a trial, why placebo and global trials turn on feasibility and post-trial duties, why commercial funding needs structural firewalls rather than mere disclosure, and why a replication crisis sharpened by AI is itself a subject-protection problem. Ethical research is not research with a signature. It is research designed so that the people who make knowledge possible — and the future patients who will rely on it — are treated as persons first.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
10.3 Review Questions
Write a brief answer to each, or use them as discussion prompts.


Recall
How does the Belmont principle of Justice translate into the concrete IRB requirement of Fair Subject Selection, and what is the difference between cognitive and allocational vulnerability in Kipnis’s taxonomy?


Apply
A biotech company wants to compile a large registry of genomic data from children in foster care to study rare pediatric neurological conditions. Consent will be obtained from state caseworkers, and the children’s data will be fully de-identified. Based on Kipnis’s types of vulnerability and the Belmont principles, identify two distinct ethical concerns that an IRB should address before approving this protocol.


Debate
In global clinical research, does the local unavailability of a proven treatment justify using a placebo-controlled design in a low-resource host country, or does this practice represent a double standard that exploits structural global inequalities?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
10.4 References

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Where We Left Off

Part A explored the biological development of the fetus alongside major candidate criteria for establishing moral status in clinical ethics.
We examined foundational moral frameworks including Judith Jarvis Thomson’s violinist analogy, Don Marquis’s future-like-ours (FLO) account, and Rosalind Hursthouse’s virtue ethics framework.
Part B transitions from pure moral theory to investigate the concrete legal, historical, and global policies that govern reproduction.
Our primary inquiry centers on how governments regulate individual reproduction, and where these state interests clash with individual bodily autonomy.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Today’s Learning Outcomes

By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

Tracing the legal trajectory of reproduction from ancient exposure practices and English Common Law quickening standards to the U.S. post-Dobbs landscape.
Analyze the foundational legal concepts of Common Law, precedent, and stare decisis as they apply to healthcare.
Compare coercive and non-coercive demographic state policies (China’s One-Child Policy vs. Romania’s Decree 770 and Singapore’s incentive package).
Apply the four principles (autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice) to post-Dobbs clinical exceptions and moral distress.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Opening Thought Experiment: The Sovereign’s Cradle
text:
Opening Thought Experiment: The Sovereign’s Cradle







Who owns the cradle?
Imagine a state facing severe demographic collapse that passes a law making pregnancy and childbirth a mandatory public service for all compatible citizens.

If a country is facing severe population collapse, does the government have a right to encourage or push citizens to have more children, or is family size a purely private choice?
Where is the boundary between a helpful public health incentive (like child care funding) and an unfair level of state control over reproduction?
How would you weigh a state’s concern for its future survival against an individual’s right to control their own body?




Use this thought experiment to set the theme: reproductive rights are not just about personal moral choices, but about the historical boundary lines between individual liberty and state authority.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Three starting points are crucial for our analysis today:

State reproductive interests are historically fluid and have shifted between demanding more births or fewer births based on economic utility.
The law functions as an ethical instrument that institutionalizes specific balances of autonomy, paternalism, and justice.
Vague statutory codes often fracture when they collide with the rapid, continuous clinical realities of emergency medicine.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Exposure & Infanticide in Antiquity


The Historical Reality

In ancient Roman and Greek societies, the abandonment or exposure of unwanted newborn infants was widely tolerated and legally practiced.
This practice served as the primary mechanism for family planning, population control, and managing congenital anomalies.




The Patria Potestas Model

Under Roman law, the male head of the household held absolute legal authority over all family members, which included the power of life and death.
A newborn did not possess inherent legal status at birth; protection was granted only when the father formally accepted the child into the household.



Point out that in antiquity, the boundary of legal and moral protection did not sit at fertilization, neural activity, or birth. It sat at paternal acceptance.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The Shift to Moral Status

The rise of Judeo-Christian ethics in the late Roman Empire led to the gradual outlawing of infant exposure and infanticide.
These moral shifts pushed the threshold of legal protection backward from paternal acceptance to the moment of birth.
The newborn was recognized for the first time as an independent legal subject owed protection against the household’s immediate utility.
This historical transition established the precedent that the state holds a fundamental duty to protect vulnerable nascent life.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
English Common Law & “Quickening”

Under English Common Law, abortion was not treated as a crime or misdemeanor prior to the biological boundary of quickening (Mohr 1978).
Quickening is defined as the moment when a pregnant person first feels the fetus move in the womb, typically occurring between 16 and 20 weeks.
Before quickening occurred, there was no objective evidence of independent life under common law understanding, meaning early termination was legally neutral.
Once quickening was documented, performing an abortion was treated as a misdemeanor rather than a capital offense.


Emphasize that the quickening standard represents a subjective, patient-centered boundary of legal status rather than an abstract biological marker.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Early American Law & Advertised Midwifery

Early American jurisdictions inherited and fully adopted the English Common Law quickening standard.
Prior to the mid-19th century, early-term abortifacient medications and services were openly advertised and sold in U.S. newspapers.
Reproductive healthcare was largely managed within the home by patients, midwives, and herbalists without formal state interference.
Before 1840, no state in the union had enacted statutory bans on early-term abortion.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The 19th-Century Professionalization Shift

The newly formed American Medical Association (AMA) launched a aggressive national campaign in 1847 to criminalize abortion at all stages (Mohr 1978).
Elite physicians utilized this campaign to establish monopoly control over medical practice, successfully squeezing out midwives and homeopaths.
Physicians argued that biological life was continuous from fertilization, making the common law quickening threshold scientifically arbitrary.
By 1880, every U.S. state had enacted statutes criminalizing abortion at all stages, shifting reproductive control from the home to state-licensed doctors.


Detail the dual motives of the AMA campaign: genuine scientific conviction about embryonic continuity alongside professional self-preservation and market control.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: History Debrief: Lessons from the Past
text:
History Debrief: Lessons from the Past

How does history complicate modern debates?

Knowing that early-stage abortion was widely accepted in early American history, does this change how we should view modern laws, or should we focus strictly on our current moral values?
How should we balance the AMA’s historical professional medical arguments against the traditional, patient-controlled care managed by midwives?
When a society decides to expand legal protection to new groups (like moving from historical infanticide to protecting newborns and fetuses), what factors usually drive that change?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Statutes vs. Common Law


Statutory Law

Written laws are enacted directly by legislative bodies such as Congress or state legislatures.
These codes are precise, prospective, and codify specific rules, prohibitions, and penalties.
An example is a state legislature passing a statutory code banning abortion after six weeks.




Common Law

Case law is developed dynamically by judges through written decisions of courts.
Rulings are iterative, retrospective, and based on the historical accumulation of decisions.
An example is the court-developed duty-to-warn standard in modern psychiatry (Tarasoff).



Intro students often confuse these two. Emphasize that the U.S. healthcare system sits at the intersection of both statutory codes and judicial common law rulings.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Precedent Defined

Legal Precedent
A precedent is a principle or rule established in a previous legal case that is either binding on or persuasive for a court when deciding subsequent cases with similar facts.


Lower courts must follow the binding precedents set by higher courts within their specific jurisdiction.
Higher courts generally follow their own prior precedents to maintain horizontal consistency and legal integrity.
Precedents allow individuals, hospitals, and clinicians to predict the legal consequences of their professional clinical actions.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The Principle of Stare Decisis

The legal principle of stare decisis translates literally to “stand by things decided.”
Under this rule, courts must adhere to prior precedents and not lightly overrule established rulings.
This conservative standard prevents the law from changing rapidly with every political shift or judicial appointment.
Adhering to prior rulings ensures that citizens can rely on the law as a stable and predictable framework over decades.


Explain that stare decisis is not an absolute rule, but a powerful judicial presumption in favor of consistency over individual correction.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: The Moral Weight of Reliance Interests
text:
The Moral Weight of Reliance Interests

Citizens develop legitimate expectations and structure their lives, educations, and careers relying on established precedents.
Over decades, individuals made long-term decisions about families and careers relying on the availability of reproductive autonomy.
Rulings that overrule long-standing precedents directly disrupt these reliance interests, particularly impacting women’s participation in public life.
To overrule a precedent, a court must justify why correcting a perceived error is worth disrupting massive reliance interests.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Overturning Precedent: The High Bar

High courts can and do overturn prior precedents if a ruling is judged fundamentally unworkable or unjust.
The court examines whether the precedent is clear enough for lower courts to apply consistently, or if it generates endless confusion.
Judges consider whether medical science, technology, or societal understanding have evolved to make the old rule obsolete.
A famous historical example is the Supreme Court overturning the segregationist precedent of Plessy v. Ferguson in Brown v. Board of Education.


Use Brown v. Board to show that overturning precedent is sometimes morally mandatory, but requires meeting a heavy burden of justification.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Precedent in Action: Casey’s Defense of Roe
text:
Precedent in Action: Casey’s Defense of Roe

In Planned Parenthood v. Casey, a newly appointed conservative-leaning court was widely expected to overrule Roe v. Wade (US Supreme Court 1992).
Justices O’Connor, Kennedy, and Souter refused to overrule Roe, basing their entire decision on stare decisis and reliance interests.
The court ruled that overruling under pressure would subvert the judiciary’s legitimacy and disrupt societal reliance on reproductive liberty.
The compromise preserved the essential holding of Roe (pre-viability access) while permitting more pre-viability regulations.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Precedent Overturned: Dobbs’ Reasoning

In Dobbs v. Jackson, the Supreme Court overruled both Roe and Casey entirely, ending fifty years of federal constitutional protection (US Supreme Court 2022).
Justice Alito argued that Roe was egregiously wrong from the day it was decided, violating originalist constitutional limits.
The majority claimed that Roe’s trimester framework was unworkable and had failed to produce a stable societal settlement.
Alito argued that because Roe withdrew protection from what many citizens consider a human person, reliance interests could not protect a wrongful precedent.


Highlight Alito’s point: stare decisis cannot be used to protect a precedent that the current court judges to involve the unjust termination of potential human life.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Legal Literacy Review

Predictability vs. Correction

In your own life, how important is it that rules remain stable and predictable over time? Why might a court choose to stand by an old decision even if it thinks the original reasoning was flawed?
How do people rely on long-standing legal rights when making major plans like choosing a career, getting married, or deciding when to start a family?
If key laws can be reversed whenever new judges are appointed, does that make the court look like a neutral legal referee or a political body? How can a legal system protect its credibility?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Three Visions of Liberty

Roe v. Wade (1973) defined liberty as privacy and autonomy under the Due Process Clause, protected by a biological trimester grid (US Supreme Court 1973).
Planned Parenthood v. Casey (1992) defined liberty as relational reliance and self-definition, protected by the “undue burden” standard (US Supreme Court 1992).
Dobbs v. Jackson (2022) defined liberty as strictly bounded by history and democratic federalism, returning reproductive control to local state legislatures (US Supreme Court 2022).


Use this slide to group these three cases conceptually. Frame them as three completely distinct philosophical definitions of what “liberty” means under the constitution.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Model 1: The Trimester Model & Biological Grid (Roe)
text:
Model 1: The Trimester Model & Biological Grid (Roe)


The Core Holding

The 14th Amendment’s liberty guarantee includes a fundamental right to privacy that covers reproductive decisions (US Supreme Court 1973).
Any state law restricting this right must meet the highest bar of constitutional review, known as strict scrutiny.




The Biological Grid

During Trimester 1, the state cannot restrict abortion; decisions rest solely with the patient and doctor.
During Trimester 2, the state may regulate abortion only to protect maternal health.
During Trimester 3 (Viability), the state’s interest in potential life becomes compelling, allowing full bans except to save maternal life.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Model 2: The Viability / Undue Burden Model (Casey)
text:
Model 2: The Viability / Undue Burden Model (Casey)

Casey abandoned the trimester grid, establishing viability as the single moral and legal threshold (US Supreme Court 1992).
Before viability, the state may regulate abortion to protect potential life, provided the regulation does not create an undue burden.
An undue burden is defined as a regulation that has the purpose or effect of placing a substantial obstacle in the path of a patient seeking an abortion.
After viability, the state’s interest is compelling, and it may ban abortion entirely, provided it has exceptions for maternal life and health.


Point out that Casey lowered the level of constitutional protection from “strict scrutiny” to “undue burden,” paving the way for targeted restrictions (TRAP laws).

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The Reliance Focus in Casey

The Casey majority emphasized that a generation of women had structured their lives and careers around reproductive autonomy.
The court argued that reproductive control is essential for women’s equal participation in the economic and social life of the nation.
Rejecting Roe under political pressure would cause deep harm by undermining the reliance interests of millions of citizens.
This focus highlights how reproductive laws directly impact structural justice and gender equality.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Model 3: The Democratic / History Model (Dobbs)

Dobbs declared that the U.S. Constitution does not confer a right to abortion, overruling Roe and Casey entirely (US Supreme Court 2022).
Justice Alito held that any constitutional right not explicitly named in the text must be “deeply rooted in this Nation’s history and tradition.”
Because 19th-century common law and state statutes criminalized abortion, Alito concluded it lacks any constitutional basis.
The authority to regulate, ban, or protect abortion is returned entirely to the elected representatives of each individual state.


Highlight that Dobbs changes the ethical forum: abortion is no longer a question of constitutional rights managed by federal courts, but a political question settled by state-by-state elections.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Comparative Grid: U.S. Constitutional Arc









Legal Model / Case
Constitutional Basis
Core Legal Threshold
State Regulatory Power




Roe v. Wade (1973)
14th Amendment Privacy
Trimesters (1st, 2nd, 3rd)
Banned in Trimester 1; regulated in 2 for health; bans allowed in 3


Planned Parenthood v. Casey (1992)
14th Amendment Liberty & Reliance
Viability (approx. 24 weeks)
Allowed pre-viability unless it poses an undue burden


Dobbs v. Jackson (2022)
Democratic Federalism / State Interest
None (Returned to States)
Unrestricted power to ban from conception with no federal right

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Constitutional Arc Debate

Where should moral questions be settled?

When a society is deeply divided on a moral issue, is it better to let the voters in each state decide through local elections, or should a national court declare a single standard that protects everyone?
If the law only protects rights that are “deeply rooted in history,” what does that mean for modern issues that our ancestors never envisioned, like modern technology or changing family structures?
How does a state-by-state legal patchwork affect fairness? Is it fair that a person’s legal rights and healthcare options change completely just by crossing a state line?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The U.S. Post-Dobbs Patchwork & 2x2 Grid


The State Landscape

The U.S. instantly fractured into highly restrictive and highly protected zones following the Dobbs decision.
Pre-existing trigger laws designed to automatically ban abortion the moment Roe was overturned took effect immediately in several states.




The 2x2 Legal Matrix

Total Bans: Abortion banned from conception with minimal exceptions (e.g., Texas, Idaho).
Gestational Limits: Restrictions at 6, 12, or 15 weeks (e.g., Florida, North Carolina).
Protected Access: Access explicitly codified in state constitutions (e.g., California, Michigan).
Shield States: Laws protecting local clinicians from out-of-state subpoenas (e.g., Massachusetts).



Explain how this patchwork creates extreme legal complexity for patients who must cross state lines to access care, and clinicians operating near borders.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The “Chilling Effect” in Clinical Practice

The chilling effect represents the inhibition or discouragement of legitimate professional clinical judgment due to fear of severe legal sanctions.
Post-Dobbs statutes in restrictive states carry severe felony penalties, including up to 99 years in prison, massive personal fines, and mandatory loss of medical license.
When faced with severe criminal prosecution, clinician attention inevitably shifts from patient welfare to personal legal defense.
Fear of prosecution forces clinicians to delay or withhold standard-of-care treatments until a patient’s condition becomes undeniably life-threatening (Harris 2022).

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Clinical Autonomy Under Threat

Clinicians are historically treated as autonomous moral agents responsible for patient welfare under the principle of beneficence.
Post-Dobbs laws strip clinicians of this professional autonomy, replacing standard clinical guidelines with statutory mandates.
Physicians in emergency departments must frequently consult hospital lawyers before performing routine medical procedures for miscarriages.
This creates a direct moral conflict between clinical duty (beneficence) and personal self-preservation (avoiding a felony).


Stress the deep moral distress this creates: doctors are trained to act early to prevent sepsis or hemorrhage, but laws force them to wait until these disasters occur.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The Ambiguity of Medical Exceptions

Most restrictive state laws include exceptions only for a “medical emergency” or “to save the life of the mother.”
Clinical deterioration is a continuous, unpredictable spectrum rather than a binary switch between healthy and dead.
Statutes do not define how close to death a patient must be—or what percentage chance of mortality they must face—to license medical intervention.
Clinicians are forced to watch patients deteriorate because intervening too early could be prosecuted as a felony.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Bedside Case 1: Ectopic Pregnancy

Ectopic Pregnancy & Delayed Care
An ectopic pregnancy occurs when a fertilized egg implants outside the uterus (usually in a fallopian tube). It is biologically incapable of surviving, and as it grows, it will inevitably rupture the tube, causing life-threatening internal hemorrhage.


The clinical standard of care demands the immediate termination of the pregnancy via surgery or medication to protect the patient’s life and fertility.
Because some state laws ban all abortions from fertilization, physicians have reportedly delayed treating ectopic pregnancies until they can document a rupture or a failing heart rate.
Forcing a patient to wait until a fallopian tube ruptures violates the principle of non-maleficence, subjecting them to avoidable trauma and risking permanent infertility.


Highlight that ectopic pregnancy has a 100% fetal mortality rate anyway, making delayed care a pure harm to the patient with zero benefit to potential life.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Bedside Case 2: Previable Preeclampsia

A patient at 20 weeks gestation develops severe preeclampsia, risking maternal stroke, kidney failure, or seizures.
At 20 weeks, the fetus is completely previable (cannot survive outside the womb), and the only medical cure is delivery.
The fetus still has a heartbeat, meaning delivery represents an abortion under restrictive state statutes.
Hospital legal teams in restrictive states have advised doctors to wait until the patient develops active organ failure or seizures before delivering, to prove they met the emergency exception.
The medical team is forced to violate beneficence, actively watching a patient under their care slide into a preventable medical catastrophe.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Clinician Moral Distress

Moral distress represents the psychological suffering experienced when a clinician knows the ethically correct action to take but is blocked from acting by legal or systemic constraints.
Pediatricians, OB-GYNs, and emergency physicians in restrictive states report unprecedented levels of anxiety, moral distress, and burnout.
Restrictive states have seen significant drops in residency applications and a flight of maternal-fetal medicine specialists, creating OB-GYN deserts.
When specialists leave, the justice burden falls heaviest on low-income patients who cannot afford to travel out of state for basic prenatal care.


Connect this slide back to the principle of Justice: the downstream effect of clinical moral distress is a structural reduction in healthcare access for the most vulnerable.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Bedside Policy Debrief

How should hospital boards respond?

Imagine you are running a hospital in a state with a strict abortion ban. How do you help doctors decide when a patient is “sick enough” to qualify for a life-saving exception without risking prison time?
If a doctor believes that waiting for legal clearance will cause permanent harm to a patient, what should they do? Does their duty of beneficence to the patient override their duty to follow the state law?
How does the threat of severe criminal penalties change the relationship between a patient and their doctor? What happens to trust when a doctor has to worry about legal liability?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Placing the U.S. in Global Context
text:
Placing the U.S. in Global Context

Global abortion laws range from total prohibition to unrestricted elective access, reflecting highly diverse cultural and political balances.
The U.S. is one of the very few developed nations to actively restrict reproductive rights in the 21st century, contrasting with a global trend toward liberalization.
Most European nations balance fetal and maternal interests through early gestational limits combined with complete universal healthcare integration.
Canada treats abortion entirely as a standard medical procedure with zero criminal statutes, relying solely on professional medical guidelines.


Use this section to broaden the debate, showing students that the U.S. culture-war framing is not the only way modern democracies organize reproductive policy.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Model A: The European Compromise

Countries like France, Germany, and Italy generally restrict elective abortion after 12 to 14 weeks.
These limits are paired with comprehensive state support: mandated impartial counseling, short waiting periods, and universal public funding.
Because abortion is fully covered by national health insurance and provided in standard clinics, it remains highly accessible, safe, and free of social stigma.
While Europe has earlier gestational limits than pre-Dobbs America, its lack of financial barriers and systemic stigma means actual access is often smoother and more equitable.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Model B: The Canadian Medical Model
text:
Model B: The Canadian Medical Model

In 1988, the Supreme Court of Canada (R. v. Morgentaler) struck down the country’s abortion law as a violation of a woman’s security of the person.
Since 1988, Canada has had no federal or provincial criminal law governing abortion whatsoever.
Abortion is regulated like any other surgical or medical procedure under provincial medical licensing boards and professional standard guidelines.
Fully funded by provincial healthcare plans, ensuring that access is distributed based on medical need rather than individual ability to pay.


Explain that Canada’s model has not led to skyrocketing late-term abortions; professional standards and clinical guidelines naturally govern the practice responsibly without criminal codes.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
China’s One-Child Policy & Coercive Anti-Natalism (1979–2015)

Fearing that rapid population growth would paralyze economic development, the Chinese government turned to extreme state coercion in the 1970s.
China’s One-Child Policy restricted urban couples to a single child, representing the most radical experiment in reproductive engineering in human history (Fong 2016).
The policy was enforced through massive financial penalties, mandatory IUD insertions, forced abortions, and state-sanctioned sterilizations.
The state treated female fertility as a collective national resource, overriding individual bodily self-determination to achieve state utility goals.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The Mechanisms of Coercion


State Surveillance

A vast network of state officials monitored women’s menstrual cycles, contraception use, and pregnancies.
Factory managers, neighborhood committees, and local informants monitored compliance, making reproductive privacy impossible (Fong 2016).




The Consequences

Women pregnant with unauthorized second children were routinely forced into clinics for terminations up to the third trimester.
The policy created a massive gender imbalance due to sex-selective abortion, alongside a rapidly shrinking labor force.



Highlight Fong’s research: the policy left China with a rapidly shrinking workforce and millions of “surplus men” who will never find partners.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
China’s Sudden Demography Pivot

Facing a rapidly shrinking labor force and an aging demographic crisis, the Chinese state abruptly ended the One-Child Policy in 2015.
The government rapidly pivoted to a Two-Child Policy in 2016, and then a Three-Child Policy in 2021.
While the rules changed, the core bioethical issue remained: individual reproduction remains treated as a tool of state economic planning.
Utilitarian calculations that treat individual lives solely as means to national economic utility inevitably violate the Kantian duty to respect persons as ends in themselves.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The Birth Dearth & Demographic Panic

Across the developed world, fertility rates have collapsed well below the replacement rate of 2.1.
Falling birth rates lead to shrinking workforces, collapsing tax bases, and underfunded social safety nets for the elderly.
Governments are increasingly shifting their focus from population control to active state interventions designed to increase birth rates.
These pro-natalist interventions range from supportive, voluntary incentives to extreme, coercive paternalism.


Use this slide to introduce the pro-natalist side of state intervention, showing that coercion can occur in both directions: forcing people not to have children, or forcing them to have them.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Non-Coercive Pro-Natalism (Support Models)

Countries like Singapore and France use non-coercive, voluntary incentives to make childbearing more attractive (Singapore National Population and Talent Division 2023).
State support includes cash baby bonuses paid directly to parents, subsidized IVF treatments, and priority access to public housing.
Governments also provide highly subsidized childcare and paid parental leave for both mothers and fathers.
These policies respect autonomy, aiming to reduce the economic and structural barriers that prevent people from having children they already desire.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Coercive Pro-Natalism: Romania’s Decree 770

Fearing economic stagnation, dictator Nicolae Ceaușescu issued Decree 770 in 1966, totally banning all abortion and contraception for women under 45 with fewer than four children (Kligman 1998).
The state treated the womb as national property, declaring that the fetus was the property of the entire society.
Secret police agents monitored hospitals; women underwent mandatory monthly gynecological exams at work to detect early pregnancies and prevent secret abortions.
The policy spurred a massive black market of unsafe illegal abortions, leading to over 10,000 maternal deaths and a massive wave of abandoned children in state orphanages.


Detail the tragic legacy of Romania’s Decree 770: it remains the most extreme modern warning of what happens when a state uses coercive pro-natalism to treat citizens as breeding stock.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Case Debrief: The Scope of State Interest
text:
Case Debrief: The Scope of State Interest

Does the state own the future?

Is there a difference between a government offering positive incentives (like tax breaks or housing priorities) to encourage childbearing and a government actively banning reproductive healthcare? Where is the ethical boundary?
If a state provides massive financial help to new parents, is that a supportive public health policy, or does it create subtle pressure that overrides personal choice?
How should a society balance its need for future generations with an individual’s right to choose a life path that does not include children?

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The Four Principles Conflict Grid








Principle
Pro-Choice / Liberal Interpretation
Pro-Life / Conservative Interpretation




Autonomy
The pregnant person holds absolute self-ownership and the right to make irreversible physical decisions about their body.
The fetus has a nascent, developing capacity for autonomy that must be protected against external termination.


Beneficence
Preserving the health, life-plans, physical safety, and socioeconomic flourishing of the pregnant person.
Preserving the primary, fundamental benefit of life itself for the developing child.


Non-Maleficence
Avoiding the severe physical, psychological, and systemic harms of forced pregnancy and delayed clinical care.
Avoiding the ultimate, irreversible harm of ending a human life (killing).


Justice
Ensuring that reproductive burdens do not fall disproportionately on low-income, marginalized, and minority patients.
Protecting the most vulnerable, defenseless, and voiceless human subject (the fetus) under the law.




Keep this slide visible for a long time. It shows that the abortion debate is not a simple battle between “morality” and “immorality,” but a clash of two internally coherent, competing interpretations of the four core principles.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
The Coherence of Moderation

Public debates often present the choice as an absolute binary between total protection from conception vs. total access up to birth.
Many bioethicists defend a gradualist model, where moral status and the right to protection grow continuously as the fetus develops.
A gradualist position protecting elective access early, but restricting it after viability with robust health exceptions is not a weak compromise.
It represents a coherent, principled attempt to balance patient autonomy (which dominates early) with state interest in potential life (which becomes compelling later as biological capacity develops).

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
section: Bridge to Case Study 4: Kate Cox
text:
Bridge to Case Study 4: Kate Cox

In 2023, a Texas mother named Kate Cox learned at 20 weeks gestation that her fetus had Trisomy 18—a severe chromosomal anomaly that is almost always fatal at or shortly after birth.
Continuing the pregnancy threatened Cox’s health and future fertility, requiring a risky third C-section.
Because Texas’s total abortion ban had extremely narrow exceptions, Cox’s physician petitioned the courts for permission to perform the termination.
The Texas Supreme Court denied the request, ruling that her condition was not yet life-threatening enough to meet the statutory exception.
In Case Study 4, we will analyze this landmark case, exploring the direct collision of bedside clinical reality and restrictive state policy.


Use this bridge to prime students for the upcoming case study. It connects the abstract legal theories of Dobbs directly to a real, highly publicized clinical emergency.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
References & Reading List

Legal Cases:

Roe v. Wade, 410 U.S. 113 (1973) (US Supreme Court 1973)
Planned Parenthood v. Casey, 505 U.S. 833 (1992) (US Supreme Court 1992)
Dobbs v. Jackson Women’s Health Organization, 597 U.S. 215 (2022) (US Supreme Court 2022)

Books & Articles:

Abortion in America: The Origins and Evolution of National Policy by James C. Mohr (1978) (Mohr 1978)
The Politics of Duplicity: Controlling Reproduction in Ceausescu’s Romania by Gail Kligman (1998) (Kligman 1998)
One Child: The Story of China’s Most Radical Experiment by Mei Fong (2016) (Fong 2016)
Navigating Loss of Abortion Access — A Clinician’s Perspective by Lisa H. Harris (2022) in NEJM (Harris 2022)






Fong, Mei. 2016. One Child: The Story of China’s Most Radical Experiment. Houghton Mifflin Harcourt.


Harris, Lisa H. 2022. “Navigating Loss of Abortion Access — a Clinician’s Perspective.” New England Journal of Medicine 387 (5): 381–83. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMp2206246.


Kligman, Gail. 1998. The Politics of Duplicity: Controlling Reproduction in Ceausescu’s Romania. University of California Press.


Mohr, James C. 1978. Abortion in America: The Origins and Evolution of National Policy, 1800–1900. Oxford University Press.


Singapore National Population and Talent Division. 2023. Enhancements to the Marriage and Parenthood Package. Singapore Government Policy Statement. https://www.strategygroup.gov.sg/.


US Supreme Court. 1973. Roe v. Wade, 410 u.s. 113. United States Reports. https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/410/113/.


US Supreme Court. 1992. Planned Parenthood of Southeastern Pennsylvania v. Casey, 505 u.s. 833. United States Reports. https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/505/833/.


US Supreme Court. 2022. Dobbs v. Jackson Women’s Health Organization, 597 u.s. 215. United States Reports. https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/597/19-1392/.

title: Lecture 4 — Abortion
text:
Part A explored the biological development of the fetus alongside major candidate criteria for establishing moral status in clinical ethics.
We examined foundational moral frameworks including Judith Jarvis Thomson’s violinist analogy, Don Marquis’s future-like-ours (FLO) account, and Rosalind Hursthouse’s virtue ethics framework.
Part B transitions from pure moral theory to investigate the concrete legal, historical, and global policies that govern reproduction.
Our primary inquiry centers on how governments regulate individual reproduction, and where these state interests clash with individual bodily autonomy.





By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

Tracing the legal trajectory of reproduction from ancient exposure practices and English Common Law quickening standards to the U.S. post-Dobbs landscape.
Analyze the foundational legal concepts of Common Law, precedent, and stare decisis as they apply to healthcare.
Compare coercive and non-coercive demographic state policies (China’s One-Child Policy vs. Romania’s Decree 770 and Singapore’s incentive package).
Apply the four principles (autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice) to post-Dobbs clinical exceptions and moral distress.












Who owns the cradle?
Imagine a state facing severe demographic collapse that passes a law making pregnancy and childbirth a mandatory public service for all compatible citizens.

If a country is facing severe population collapse, does the government have a right to encourage or push citizens to have more children, or is family size a purely private choice?
Where is the boundary between a helpful public health incentive (like child care funding) and an unfair level of state control over reproduction?
How would you weigh a state’s concern for its future survival against an individual’s right to control their own body?





Use this thought experiment to set the theme: reproductive rights are not just about personal moral choices, but about the historical boundary lines between individual liberty and state authority.










Note



Three starting points are crucial for our analysis today:

State reproductive interests are historically fluid and have shifted between demanding more births or fewer births based on economic utility.
The law functions as an ethical instrument that institutionalizes specific balances of autonomy, paternalism, and justice.
Vague statutory codes often fracture when they collide with the rapid, continuous clinical realities of emergency medicine.

Part B transitions from pure moral theory to investigate the concrete legal, historical, and global policies that govern reproduction.
Our primary inquiry centers on how governments regulate individual reproduction, and where these state interests clash with individual bodily autonomy.

text:
Who owns the cradle?
Imagine a state facing severe demographic collapse that passes a law making pregnancy and childbirth a mandatory public service for all compatible citizens.

If a country is facing severe population collapse, does the government have a right to encourage or push citizens to have more children, or is family size a purely private choice?
